Dependency management systems have caused the biggest yaks I've ever shaved, and those couple of hours of annoyance are a small drop in the bucket.
After going through the irritation of changing 4 projects for 1 line of code, he understood my earlier cursing.
Before he sits down to write this re-encoding of everything, we ask our email marketers how they did this in the past.

I couldn't hack the dependency problems, and hacking the code restructuring would have resulted in threading an arbitrary parameter through a bunch of unrelated code just to avoid thinking about the right solution.
The upside of the yak shaving was more data, a better code base for making such changes in the future, and a recognition of a serious dependency structure problem that we need to address.
posts.key_insight: Knowing when to embrace necessary yak-shaving for long-term code health versus when to apply a simple hack requires judgment that comes from understanding both the technical debt and the actual problem being solved.

posts.summary: Hack days are valuable for innovation and experimentation, but they create a dangerous illusion about project complexity. The author describes how hack day successes at Rent the Runway led to dramatically underestimated timelines - an '80% complete' rewrite was actually 80% of basic functionality, ignoring half the required features. Similarly, a cool reviews project that took one day to hack became a man-year engineering effort. While acknowledging her own pragmatic approach (shipping tested, production-ready code), she still endorses the value of ambitious hack day moonshots.
key_points: Hack day projects skip tests, edge cases, and scalability concerns, creating a false sense of completion
The '80% done' illusion is particularly dangerous - it often means 80% of basic functionality, ignoring the harder half of requirements
Cool hack day projects can seduce teams into dramatically underestimating production timelines (one day hack → 4 engineers for 3 months)
Engineers can fall so in love with hacks that they neglect regular duties for projects with questionable business value
There's value in pragmatic hack day projects that ship same-day with tests, but moonshot projects still have their place
money_quotes: We can't forget the seductive illusion of the hack.
In reality, the 80% completion was more like '80% of basic functionality completed', which is still very impressive but ignores the 50% of required functionality that goes well beyond basic.
I don't think any of us expected this cool thing that took a day to hack to turn into 4 engineers working for three full months just to get a polished v1.
The hack day siren song can also cause you to fall in love with your hack so much you don't see the cost to making it production-ready.
It's hard to complain when people are working on something they're passionate about, but it's important for a team to feel like they are all working toward the same goal.
My expected 2 hour hack still took me closer to 6 hours end to end.
What's the point in fast-loading images if no one ever views them?
posts.key_insight: Hack day projects create a dangerous illusion of near-completion that can lead teams to dramatically underestimate the true effort required for production-ready software.

posts.summary: The startup industry suffers from a leadership deficit precisely because its core values—speed, agility, and lean operations—conflict with the slow, deliberate work of developing people leaders. The 'myth of the natural leader' leads companies to promote articulate engineers without giving them actual management training, even though management is a learned skill requiring practice in communication, conflict resolution, and planning. The author argues that leadership is best learned through apprenticeship rather than books or external advisors, drawing on her own experience of hours of one-on-one mentorship. She proposes that the industry adopt a standard requiring senior engineers to have hands-on mentoring and leadership experience before promotion.
key_points: Management is a learned skill, not a natural talent—promoting smart communicators without training sets them up to fail
Leadership is best developed through apprenticeship models with direct mentorship, not just books or external advisors
Startup culture's focus on speed and hustle actively works against the time investment needed to develop leaders
The industry disparages MBAs but offers no alternative channel for developing equivalent management skills
Requiring leadership experience for senior engineer titles would ensure every growing company has people with management fundamentals
money_quotes: The dirty, tiring work of people leadership is often at odds with the hustle of startup life.
There is very little that is natural about team management.
We often disparage the MBA, but we do very little to replicate that skill channel in our own industry.
I've learned most of my leadership skills thanks to hours of one-on-one time with current and former bosses, walking through presentations and plans, discussing personnel issues, perfecting pitches.
Let's stop promoting people to the role of senior engineer who have never had the experience of hands-on direction of people and projects.

If someone has five to ten years of experience writing software in teams, and they have never had the responsibility of mentoring junior developers in a meaningful way, they are lacking something in their skill set.
We know that software is humans.
If the title of senior engineer recognizes that you can deliver, it should also recognize that you can handle what happens when you do.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's leadership shortage is self-inflicted: we mock formal management education while providing no apprenticeship alternative, then act surprised when promoted engineers can't lead.

posts.summary: Fournier reflects on the tension between technical correctness and practical outcomes in engineering decisions. She recounts fighting her boss over an API design choice she knew was right, only to later find herself as a manager making the same pragmatic tradeoffs with her own developers. The post explores how being technically correct often matters less than shipping, building consensus, and driving business results. She acknowledges the frustration engineers feel when overruled, while explaining why experienced leaders choose pragmatism over rightness.
key_points: Being technically right doesn't automatically mean the fight to prove it is worth having
The time spent debating correctness often exceeds the value of the correct solution
Managers must weigh technical decisions against non-technical factors like adoption, consensus, and business impact
Engineers often don't believe their managers truly understand their technical position, even when they do
Experience tends to shift perspective from fighting for rightness to optimizing for results
money_quotes: I told him we shouldn't negotiate with terrorists.
But he also used it, which was all that mattered to my boss, and in retrospect, was more important for the success of the project than my sense of rightness.
No matter how much I say, you're right, but it doesn't matter, it doesn't sink in.
They tell me that they understand that technical decisions are sometimes a series of non-technical tradeoffs, but in this case, why can't I see that this is just the right way to do it?
You will be worn down from fighting for right, and you will be driven to produce the best results with the most consensus and not distract everyone with the overhead of debating every decision.
I still resent my boss for not fighting for me, but I understand where she was coming from.
posts.key_insight: Technical correctness is often less important than adoption, consensus, and business outcomes—a lesson engineers only truly internalize when they become managers themselves.

posts.summary: While software development is largely an art focused on architectural and design decisions, debugging and performance analysis require a scientific approach. Good debuggers treat problems methodically, forming hypotheses and testing assumptions rather than relying on trial and error. The author illustrates this through her own debugging practice of keeping lab-notebook-style notes and through a cautionary tale about performance tuning that failed due to lack of reproducible measurements. Only after establishing proper metrics could her team make confident progress on performance improvements.
key_points: Most application development decisions are judgment calls (art), but debugging and performance analysis require scientific method
Good debuggers believe in findable root causes and break down problems methodically rather than using trial and error
Performance tuning without reproducible measurements leads to guesswork and wasted time
Instinct in debugging is valuable but ultimately derives from building a foundation of scientific observation
Keeping notes resembling a lab notebook is essential for debugging complex distributed systems
money_quotes: Despite the study of computing being termed 'Computer Science', most working software developers will agree that development is more art than science.

Good debuggers, on the other hand, know that there is a root cause for errors that can be tracked down, and they are able to break down the problem in a methodical way, checking assumptions and observing behavior through repeated experiments and analysis.
I always believe that with enough patience and help I can in fact find the root of the problem, and I pretty much always do.
When I dig into a tough problem, I start keeping notes that resemble a slightly disorganized lab notebook.
We were looking for luck, but we need science, and our lack of scientific approach hampered our progress.
Good instincts start from science and cold, hard, repeatable, observable truths.
posts.key_insight: Debugging and performance tuning require scientific rigor—forming hypotheses, creating reproducible experiments, and tracking observations—rather than the trial-and-error approach many developers default to.

posts.summary: Fournier reflects on her unexpected transition from team lead to head of engineering after her boss's departure. Despite her initial confidence that she could handle the role, she quickly discovered that leading 20 people and owning organizational outcomes is fundamentally different from managing a small team. She learned that her words and moods now ripple throughout the entire organization, and that recruiting requires a completely different level of ownership. Her mother's simple advice to 'smile more' became her key strategy for projecting calm and enabling her team to thrive.
key_points: The jump from managing 6 people to leading 20 involves exponentially more responsibility, not just incrementally more
Without a buffer above you, every gap in your leadership is felt directly by your team
As the person in charge, your stress, impatience, and snide comments echo and amplify throughout the organization
Owning recruiting end-to-end is completely different from just helping with interviews
Projecting positivity through something as simple as smiling can transform team dynamics and enable people to step up
money_quotes: Call it the Dunning-Kruger effect, or simply call it arrogance, but I think if you had asked me before I was put into this position whether I could do the job well, I would have told you certainly yes.
When there is no buffer in your department above you that people can go to when your guidance is lacking, the weight of that responsibility is 10 times what you ever expected.
A sudden transition of leadership even in a solid organization such as ours stirs up long-simmering conflicts.
When I speak harshly to people over technical matters, it is yelling even if I don't intend it to be.
One snide comment about a decision or a design invites others to sneer at that decision along with me.
When I smile, I see people step up and they take on bigger responsibilities than they've ever had, and knock them out of the park over and over again.
Because change is scary and hard, but in the long run, it's good.
posts.key_insight: The transition to senior leadership reveals that your emotional state and casual comments now echo throughout the entire organization, making self-awareness and intentional positivity essential leadership skills.

posts.summary: The author shares advice from her CEO about prioritizing the 3 big hairy problems on your to-do list over the 97 easy ones. She applies this wisdom to her own transition from engineer to engineering leader, arguing that tackling your hardest challenges—not completing easy tasks—is what drives growth. The painful process of delegating beloved technical work to focus on management, strategy, and leadership has accelerated both her development and her team's growth. The easy stuff can always be done by someone else; the hard problems are what matter.
key_points: Completing easy tasks creates a false sense of productivity while avoiding what actually matters
Growth comes from immersing yourself in your stretch areas, not staying comfortable with existing skills
Delegating work you're good at benefits both you and the person who needs to learn those skills
The transition from individual contributor to leader requires painful letting go of beloved technical work
When you tackle hard problems, it creates a cascade effect that helps your whole team level up
money_quotes: Most people, when faced with such a list, will find the 97 that are obvious and easy and knock them off before worrying about the 3 big hairy things on the list. Don't do that. The 97 aren't worth your time, it's the 3 big hairy things that matter.
You get better faster by tackling the hardest problems you have and ignoring the rest.
Delegate the things that are easy for you (read: the little things you do to feel good about your own productivity) to someone who still needs to learn those skills.
Being an ok manager and a great engineer is no longer enough for me to do my job. I need to be an excellent manager, an inspirational leader, a great strategist and a savvy planner.
I would call the last 3 months being mostly out of coding and in planning/management/recruiting land to be some of the hardest of my career.
A savvy entrepreneur knows that the easy stuff can always be done by someone else, so let someone else do it. The hard problems are the problems that matter.
posts.key_insight: Professional growth accelerates when you deliberately tackle your hardest challenges and delegate the comfortable work you've already mastered to others who need to learn it.

posts.summary: Rent the Runway faces a significant business challenge: women are hesitant to rent dresses because of fit uncertainty, especially with items arriving close to their events. While engineers typically propose algorithmic solutions like 3D modeling and virtual fit assistants, these technologies have failed to gain traction because they strip the emotional element from the shopping experience. The company discovered through data that photos of real women wearing dresses are far more effective than technical solutions. This insight led to the launch of 'Our Runway,' a feature letting women shop by viewing other real customers in the dresses, triggering emotional connection rather than algorithmic confidence.
key_points: Engineers instinctively jump to algorithmic solutions (3D modeling, computer vision) for fit problems, but these have consistently failed to gain user adoption
Virtual fit technology falls into the uncanny valley - it undermines rather than builds confidence because it doesn't look enough like real people
The fit problem is fundamentally emotional, not algorithmic - women need to feel good about how something will look, not just know measurements
Photos of real women wearing dresses are more effective because the human brain excels at visual tasks and emotional identification
Data-driven insights combined with emotional reasoning produced a better solution than pure technical approaches
money_quotes: What engineers often forget when faced with a problem is the emotional element of that problem.
Fit seems like an algorithmic problem, but for many women, there is a huge emotional component to trying things on.
Turning fit into a cheesy 3D model strips all emotion from the experience, and puts it into the uncanny valley of not-quite-realness.
Don't forget that the human brain is still much more powerful than computers at visual tasks.
It is much easier for us to imagine ourselves in an item of clothing when we see it on many other women.
Learning to use emotional reasoning was a revelation to me, and it might be the most valuable engineering insight I've picked up in the last year.
posts.key_insight: The best engineering solutions often require understanding the emotional dimensions of a problem rather than jumping to purely technical approaches.

posts.summary: This post describes the architecture for building a globally distributed service discovery system using ZooKeeper. The core challenge is creating a system that spans three global regions (New York, London, Asia) while maintaining high availability even if an entire data center fails. The solution involves creating three regional ZooKeeper clusters, each with nodes split across local data centers plus one node in the nearest remote region to ensure quorum availability. The client wrapper implements 'Advertise Locally, Lookup Globally' - writes happen only to local clusters while reads can fall back to remote clusters. The post also covers operational lessons about running ZooKeeper as a shared service, including risks from misbehaving clients and resource limits.
key_points: ZooKeeper requires n/2+1 nodes for quorum, so surviving a data center loss requires splitting across 3 data centers minimum
Write speed degrades significantly when quorum votes must travel over WAN, making global clusters impractical
The solution uses 3 regional clusters with N-1 local nodes plus 1 remote node, ensuring the remote node never becomes leader
Client wrapper enables local writes (ephemeral nodes) with global reads (automatic fallback to remote clusters)
ZooKeeper as a shared service is risky - misbehaving clients can take down entire clusters through floods, memory exhaustion, or socket exhaustion
money_quotes: ZooKeeper requires a majority (n/2 + 1) of servers to be available and able to communicate with each other in order to form a quorum, and thus you cannot split a quorum across two data centers and guarantee that the quorum will be available with the loss of any one data center.
Write speed dramatically decreases when the quorum must wait for votes to travel over the WAN.
ZooKeeper as a Service (a shared ZooKeeper cluster maintained by a centralized infrastructure team to support many different clients) is a risky proposition.
It is easy for a misbehaving client to take down an entire cluster by flooding it with requests or making too many connections.
A long full GC pause can cause their session to the ZooKeeper cluster to time out and thus their advertisement will be deleted.
I often get the question of how to set the heartbeats, timeouts, etc, to optimize a ZooKeeper cluster, and the answer is really that it depends on your network.
posts.key_insight: Building globally distributed coordination systems requires carefully balancing quorum requirements, network latency, and failure domains - often solved by regional clusters with cross-region quorum members rather than a single global cluster.

posts.summary: The author argues that making it easy for people to do the right thing is more effective than enforcement or intimidation. Drawing from her experience as a self-appointed 'build cop' on a 40+ person team, she learned that nagging developers about broken builds was ineffective when the test suite took 4+ hours to run. By investing in infrastructure improvements—parallel tests, remote execution, in-memory databases—compliance naturally increased. She extends this principle to API design, warning against REST purism that prioritizes computer-brain logic over human readability and error prevention.
key_points: Intimidation and enforcement have limited effectiveness when the 'right thing' is genuinely difficult to do
Investing in making correct behavior easier yields better results than policing incorrect behavior
REST purity and similar standards can make systems harder for humans to use correctly when taken to extremes
Making things 'easy' doesn't mean removing all complexity—it means ensuring the easy path leads to understanding and correct usage
Human nature should be factored into system and process design, not fought against
money_quotes: One of my overriding principles is: make it easy for people to do the right thing.
People were not malicious, but the tests took a LONG time to run (upwards of 4 hours at the worst), and on the older desktops you couldn't even get much work done while the test suite ran.
All my intimidation and bullying wasn't going to cause perfect compliance.
Any quest for purity that ignores human readability does so at its peril.
I generally prefer to use frameworks that force me and my team to do more implementation work rather than making it trivially easy.
Many great engineers are tripped up by human nature, when there's really no need to be.
Look at your critical human-involving processes and think: am I making it easy for people to do the right thing here?
posts.key_insight: Systems and processes should be designed around human nature rather than expecting people to overcome friction through willpower—invest in making the right path the easy path.

posts.summary: Fournier argues against adopting git-flow branching models simply because Git makes branching easy. She explains that git-flow was designed for large distributed open source teams with long development cycles, but most modern software teams sit together, release frequently, and benefit from continuous integration. Feature branching isolates developers, delays integration problems, and encourages all-or-nothing thinking about features when incremental, flagged releases work better for web deployments. She advocates for simpler workflows with just master and release branches rather than cargo-culting complex processes.
key_points: Git was designed for distributed open source teams with long release cycles, not co-located teams doing frequent releases
Feature branching isolates developers, delays code review and integration, and makes merge conflicts worse when everyone branches
Teams deploying to websites should learn to release code incrementally behind feature flags rather than thinking in all-or-nothing feature chunks
The git-flow develop branch and pristine master requirements add unnecessary complexity when simple tagging suffices
Just because a tool does something well doesn't mean that feature is important for your team's workflow
money_quotes: There are reasons beyond tool support that would lead one to want to encourage or discourage branching and merging, and mere tool support is not reason enough to embrace a branch-driven workflow.
A developer working on a branch is working alone.
Worst of all, it's easy for them to work in the dark, shielded from the eyes of other developers.
The burden of doing the right thing is entirely on the developer and good developers are lazy (or busy, or both).
From a workflow perspective, I want something that makes small problems come to light very early and obviously to the whole team, enabling inherent communication.
Feature branching also encourages thinking about code and features as all or none.
Learning how to write code in such a way as to be chunkable, flaggable, and almost always safe to go into production is a necessary skill set for frequent releases of any sort.
Just because a tool offers a feature, and does it well, does not mean that feature is actually important for your team.
posts.key_insight: Teams should choose development workflows based on their actual needs—team structure, release frequency, and communication patterns—not because a popular tool makes a particular approach easy.

posts.summary: The author examines when ZooKeeper is appropriate versus overkill for distributed systems, framing it as a fundamental question architects must answer. She observes a clear divide: platforms like Hadoop, Storm, and Kafka use ZooKeeper for coordination, while distributed databases like MongoDB and Cassandra implement their own logic. Drawing an analogy to how RDBMSs optimize by bypassing OS functionality, she argues that databases have competitive incentives to own their coordination logic. However, for most systems, ZooKeeper represents a necessary 'operating system' component for distributed computing, since building correct distributed coordination from scratch requires expertise most teams lack.
key_points: Distributed systems split into two camps: those using ZooKeeper as a service (Hadoop, Storm, Kafka) and those implementing internal coordination (MongoDB, Riak, Cassandra)
Distributed databases have competitive incentives to own coordination logic—simpler deployment and tuned performance for their specific use cases
Successful distributed coordination requires more than a Paxos library; it demands upfront HA design, proper quorum deployment, and understanding how GC and other system behaviors affect coordination
Even Google chose to build Chubby as a service rather than a client library because most developers don't naturally think in distributed coordination terms
For most systems outside specialized databases, implementing custom coordination makes as little sense as writing a custom filesystem for a web app
money_quotes: The answer that I prefer to give is almost always 'yes, but keep these things in mind before you roll it out for this purpose.'
ZooKeeper, or a service like it, is a necessary core component of the 'operating system' for distributed computing.
It does not make sense for most systems to implement their own distributed coordination, any more than it makes sense to implement your own file system to run your RESTful web app.
Installing and administering one type of thing (the database itself) is by definition simpler than installing and administering 2 things.
Even with such a library, you would need to design your application up-front to think about high availability.
It makes sense to prefer to burn your own developer dollars to engineer around the edge cases, so as to make a simpler product for your customers.
posts.key_insight: ZooKeeper should be viewed as a fundamental operating system service for distributed computing—most teams should use it rather than reinvent coordination logic, but specialized databases have legitimate reasons to build their own.

posts.summary: Fournier argues that hackdays serve as an excellent metric for measuring the success of development platform migrations. Rent the Runway spent two years migrating from a complex Drupal monolith to Java services with a Ruby frontend, primarily to improve development velocity rather than just handle load. Their first post-migration hackday produced dramatically better results than previous ones—over ten production-worthy projects versus mostly non-functional simulations. The difference wasn't team effort or intelligence, but rather that the new platform enabled rather than resisted creative development.
key_points: Replatforming for development scalability is harder to measure than replatforming for load, but hackday output provides a concrete metric
The incremental 'hollowing out' approach—gradually replacing pieces while keeping the business running—proved effective for a major platform migration
A good platform enables developers to accomplish meaningful work without requiring complete system knowledge
The quality difference in hackday output directly reflects whether your architecture supports or fights against developer productivity
money_quotes: It's pretty common for teams, especially startups, to get to a point with their tech stack where the platform they've been working on just isn't cutting it anymore.
We wanted to burn the whole thing to the ground and start fresh.
We didn't do this, of course. We were running a successful business on that old hideous platform.
The team didn't work any less hard, nor was it any less smart than it is now. The major difference between that hack day and now is that we've replatformed our system.
Now adding new pages to the site is simple and doesn't require full knowledge of all the legacy Drupal.

You may be surprised what you learn, and you'll almost certainly be impressed with what your team can accomplish when given creative freedom and a platform that doesn't resist every attempt at creativity.
posts.key_insight: A hackday's output serves as a concrete, measurable indicator of whether a platform migration has successfully improved development velocity—if the same team produces dramatically better results, the replatforming worked.

posts.summary: After a year of serious management experience, the author reflects on the profound difficulty of transitioning from individual contributor to managing multiple teams and managers. She explores the emotional toll of compassionate leadership, the loss of technical mastery, and the uncomfortable feeling of being a beginner again after years of expertise. The post grapples with why competent people become managers despite losing autonomy and mastery, concluding that the motivation must come from a sense of purpose and desire for broader impact than individual contribution allows.
key_points: The transition from managing small teams to managing managers is far more challenging than expected, requiring emotional intelligence that technical work never demanded
Compassionate management requires caring about people without taking their actions personally - every resignation feels like a personal failure
Engineers who become managers sacrifice both autonomy and mastery, which explains why some gravitate toward detached, chess-piece approaches to leadership
The tech industry undervalues management skills while still gatekeeping with technical interviews, even for management roles
Purpose and desire for broader impact must be the driving motivation, since management sacrifices the comfort of competence
money_quotes: A successful manager needs to care about her people without taking the things they do personally.
Every person who quits feels like an indictment of all the ways you failed them.

If you wonder why it seems sometimes that management roles get taken up by sociopaths, just think that for every interaction you have with a difficult coworker, your manager has probably had to deal with ten of them.
I am a beginner all over again when it comes to the big-league management game, and it's discouraging.
I miss doing what I'm good at, building systems, and I'm afraid that I've given it up for something I may never do particularly well.
When you've spent ten plus years getting really, really good at designing and developing systems, and you leave that to think about people all day? It's hard.
I can become the greatest manager in the world, but if I wanted to work in that role at Google they would still give me highly technical interviews.
I want to have a bigger impact than I will ever be able to have as an architect or developer.
posts.key_insight: The hardest part of becoming a manager isn't the new responsibilities but the loss of mastery and certainty that comes from leaving behind a decade of hard-won technical expertise to become a beginner again.

posts.summary: The post addresses a common misconception about service-oriented architecture: that SOA necessarily creates silos between development teams. Fournier argues that at smaller companies, SOA should enable 'internal open source' where developers work across services as needed to build features. She contrasts this with large company approaches where service ownership creates intentional boundaries for data governance. The key is to architect both your systems and your teams to avoid unintended separation, whether through cross-functional business-focused teams, microservices, or unified technology stacks.
key_points: SOA is not designed to separate developers from each other; systems should be treated as 'internal open source' where any team can contribute
Large companies may intentionally use SOA to create ownership boundaries for data quality, accepting velocity tradeoffs that startups cannot afford
Language differences between services (e.g., Java backend, Ruby frontend) naturally segregate teams, requiring deliberate counter-measures
Business-focused teams with both backend and frontend developers help overcome the separation problem
The 'separation myth' makes developers reluctant to adopt SOA, but proper team architecture can avoid this pitfall
money_quotes: SOA is not designed to separate your developers from each other.

The systems are 'internal open source'. You may not be the expert in that system, but when a feature is needed, it's expected that the team creating the feature will roll up their sleeves and get coding.
That is not the way a small startup should approach SOA, but if you don't anticipate this it may become an unintended outcome.
We address this separation by working in business-focused teams that have both backend and frontend developers.
SOA is a powerful model for creating scalable software, but many developers are reluctant to adopt it because of this separation myth.
Acknowledging the risk here and architecting your teams with an eye to avoiding it is important in successfully adopting this model in an agile environment.
posts.key_insight: Service-oriented architecture should enable cross-team collaboration rather than create silos, but achieving this requires deliberately designing both your technical architecture and team structure together.

posts.summary: The post argues that engineers and managers often make the mistake of focusing on technical skill improvement when they should be developing leadership capabilities. While early-career engineers should focus on coding and technical skills, senior engineers and managers need to shift toward becoming 'multipliers' who enhance their teams through teaching, process improvement, and strategic thinking. Managers who cling to coding are often bottlenecks, and the real growth path is stepping back to observe and fix problems at a macro level. The key transition is from additive individual contribution to multiplicative team impact.
key_points: Junior engineers add value by doing tasks; senior engineers and managers add value by multiplying their team's effectiveness
Very few engineers write code of such volume and complexity that coding alone enhances the entire organization
Managers who insist on writing lots of code often become overworked bottlenecks rather than effective leaders
The solution isn't for managers to carve out coding time, but to not become a manager until coding is second nature
Growing into leadership requires getting out of your comfort zone and focusing on what makes you a multiplier
money_quotes: What got us here won't get us there.
After a certain point, it is more important to focus on what will make you a multiplier on the team.
Very very few engineers write code of such volume and complexity that simply by writing code they enhance the entire organization.
Managers that stubbornly hold onto the idea that they must write a lot of code are often either overworked, bottlenecks, or both.
Don't get pushed into management until you've spent enough time coding that it is second nature.
If you think that writing more code is the unlock for you to manage your team better, to grow as a better leader, you're going backwards.
You're not going to code your team out of crunch mode by yourself, so spend your time preventing them from getting into crunch mode in the first place.
posts.key_insight: The transition from individual contributor to leader requires shifting focus from personal technical output to becoming a multiplier who enhances the entire team's effectiveness.

posts.summary: The author argues that warnings about Moore's Law ending have become a tired refrain that no longer carries weight after decades of repetition. She points out that developers who care about performance are already writing code that handles the supposed threats—parallelization, distributed systems, and leveraging new hardware. The post contends that modern performance concerns are far more nuanced than raw processor speed, encompassing network latency, I/O bottlenecks, and data volume. Rather than fearing some apocalyptic end to Moore's Law, practical engineers have been continuously adapting their approaches as technology evolves.
key_points: Moore's Law warnings have been repeated for over a decade without the predicted catastrophic impact materializing
Developers who care about performance are already writing parallel and distributed code—they don't wait for crisis
The 'circle of tech life' means continuously adopting new approaches (parallelism, distributed systems, SSDs) as they become available
Modern performance concerns are multifaceted: network sensitivity, I/O bounds, data volume—not just processor speed
The bogeyman of Moore's Law ending is unnecessary fear-mongering for people already doing performance-conscious work
money_quotes: For as long as I can remember, Moore's Law has been one of the great tech bogeymen.
When you hear the same message for well over ten years, its efficacy starts to fade a bit.
The truth of the matter is this: if you care about Moore's Law, you're probably already writing code that combats it, because you care about performance.
The circle of tech life takes advantage of the latest hotness as needed to get the job done.
These days most of us are concerned about a much more complex interaction of performance issues than simple processor speed.
He's sitting in the next cube, making sure we parallelized all our outgoing requests, and he'd rather you stopped getting so hysterical on his behalf.
posts.key_insight: Threatening developers with Moore's Law ending is pointless because those who care about performance are already adapting continuously, and modern performance challenges extend far beyond processor speed.

posts.summary: The post revisits Yishan Wong's influential advice about promoting managers from within and argues that companies often fail because they overlook the critical requirement of mentoring new managers. Fournier observes that startups promote technical contributors to management roles but provide no guidance, expecting great managers to emerge naturally. She reflects on her own journey, admitting that despite confidence in her technical skills and leadership inclination, she only began understanding management's complexity after extensive coaching. The core argument is that promotion from within only works if paired with intensive mentorship and accountability for new managers' development.
key_points: Companies adopt 'promote from within' but skip the essential mentoring component that makes it successful
Great managers are made through patient teaching, observation of successes and failures, and being held accountable for their mistakes
Startup culture's reverence for founders and early employees often prevents the honest feedback new managers need
The author's own experience shows that even strong technical skills and natural leadership inclination are insufficient without coaching
Without proper support, promoting from within offers no advantage over hiring experienced external managers
money_quotes: Great managers are truly not born. They are made, and usually made through both being sat down and patiently taught the ways to effectively lead projects and people, but also through observing both successes and failures.
The cult of personality around founders and early employees can work against the one thing necessary to make 'promote from within' successful: some form of external help.
I came into this job two years ago thinking that given my natural willingness to be in charge and my strong technical skills I would be a great manager. Haha!
Truthfully I'm only now getting to the point where I have an inkling of all the things I don't know, and a large part of that is thanks to a ton of coaching.
We've taken these ideas that Yishan so eloquently voiced, that culture is paramount, and elevated them to high status, while forgetting that there is a lot to learn to be a successful manager.
Don't cheap out on the process by forgetting that these new managers and leaders need help, need training, need to be held responsible for both the good and the bad.
posts.key_insight: Promoting managers from within only succeeds when paired with intensive mentorship and accountability—without external coaching and honest feedback, internal promotions fail just as often as external hires risk cultural misalignment.

posts.summary: Fournier reflects on how her natural tendency toward enforcing rules and holding people accountable, which worked well as a peer, has become problematic as she's moved up the management chain. She identifies three key issues: it causes team members to stop holding each other accountable, it's demoralizing when correction comes from a senior leader, and she's now too far removed from day-to-day work to know what rules make sense. She concludes that her role should shift from rule-making and enforcement to setting high-level goals and empowering teams to create their own practices.
key_points: When a boss enforces accountability, team members stop holding each other accountable because they view it as 'the boss's job'
Corrective feedback from senior leaders is amplified and more demoralizing than the same feedback from peers
Leaders become too removed from daily work to know which rules and practices actually make sense for the current team
High-performing teams hold each other accountable; feedback should come quickly and naturally from peers
The leader's job is to set high-level goals and let teams iterate on processes until they find what works for them
money_quotes: A not-so-secret aspect of my personality is that I have no problem with the policing aspects of leadership.
It seems that having me hold individuals accountable ends up causing some individuals to stop holding each other accountable.
It is still hard to get used to the idea that I am not just one of the team, and that everything I do is amplified and taken in ways I don't always anticipate.
It is important that I'm seen as an inspirational figure to my team, someone they look up to and look forward to interacting with, and not vice-versa.
The only true best practice out there is to let your team take a concept or process and iterate on it until it takes on a form that works effectively for them.
I'm getting out of the business of being the rule maker and rule enforcer.
I want to work myself out of a job, after all, and the best ideas don't come from me, so why should the policing?
posts.key_insight: As leaders rise in seniority, their role must shift from enforcing accountability to creating conditions where teams hold themselves accountable.

posts.summary: The author reflects on her transition from tech lead to engineering manager, arguing that the skills that make someone a great tech lead don't automatically translate to management success. She describes how her early management effectiveness relied on being hands-on and teaching engineers directly, but as her role grew, she had to develop entirely new skills like running effective 1-1s, identifying systemic issues secondhand, and managing projects she hadn't personally coded. Despite extensive coaching and training, she admits to making many mistakes, and emphasizes that growing a great engineering leadership team requires deliberate structure and support, not just promoting technically skilled individuals.
key_points: Being a great tech lead and being a great manager require fundamentally different skill sets
Most startup managers struggle because they were promoted for technical excellence without learning management fundamentals
Management skills like goal-setting, hiring plans, measuring efficiency, and strategic roadmapping must be explicitly learned
Even experienced tech leads promoted to management need structured support and training to succeed
Companies must create deliberate structures for engineering managers to be successful rather than expecting natural progression
money_quotes: Being a manager is nothing at all like being a tech lead, and hiring managers on the basis of their strength as individual contributors is not the guaranteed way to great technical leadership.
Tech lead stopped being my job long before I learned how to truly manage well.
If your company doesn't provide you with a ton of structure and guidance or experienced managers to learn from, you've got a long road ahead of you if you want to become a great manager.
It is not just about charm and engineering skills, and that is probably how you got here.
You can identify process bottlenecks, but can you identify them when you are not personally impacted by them?
The great tech leads may have an easier time winning over the engineers, but they still need to be taught the basics of structured management, and they won't all be successful or happy in that role.
Engineering management is not just being the tech lead of bigger and bigger teams, and the faster you realize that, the better off your team will be.
posts.key_insight: Engineering management is a distinct discipline from technical leadership, and organizations must deliberately invest in teaching management skills rather than assuming great engineers will naturally become great managers.

posts.summary: The post argues that new engineering managers often focus too heavily on solving immediate problems while neglecting the crucial leadership skill of articulating a vision for their team's future. Through a conversation with a tech lead, Fournier identifies that technical managers rarely spend time thinking about or communicating where their team should be headed beyond the product roadmap. She distinguishes between management (handling today's reality) and leadership (providing hope through vision), noting that even excellent managers cannot inspire true team loyalty without the latter. The solution is simple but not easy: deliberately practice thinking about and articulating the future.
key_points: Moving from individual contributor to manager is a career change, not just a promotion, and requires developing new skills beyond solving technical problems
Great management skills alone cannot inspire team loyalty and passion—leadership and vision are essential additions
Technical managers are comfortable with reality but often avoid articulating future vision because it feels risky and uncertain
Leadership can be learned through deliberate practice: stepping away from daily work to think about and articulate a future vision
The most important thing a manager can do is regularly communicate not just what the team is doing, but where it's going
money_quotes: You can be the greatest manager in the world, but without leadership and vision, your team will not be truly sticky to you.
Define reality, give hope.
Too often first-time tech managers focus on reality. We're comfortable with reality, reality is our bread and butter.
When you focus on nothing but today's problems, even if you are a great manager who does everything right, you are unlikely to motivate your team to greatness.
Reacting to today is so easy, and trying to predict the future seems really hard.
Fortunately, leadership is not a skill you have to be born with. It just requires that you identify the future and articulate it.
Get away from your keyboard. Force yourself to sit in an empty room with a whiteboard or a pen and paper and write some ideas down.
posts.key_insight: The transition from technical contributor to effective leader requires moving beyond solving today's problems to deliberately practicing the articulation of a compelling future vision for your team.

posts.summary: Fournier challenges the engineering belief in meritocracy, arguing that flat organizations without titles don't create fairness but instead enable shadow hierarchies that exclude outsiders. Rather than eliminating structure, leaders should explicitly define what each level requires and make those expectations transparent to everyone. She advocates for promotion committees composed of senior engineers rather than manager-driven promotions, which creates peer accountability and buy-in. The post acknowledges this approach requires significant work but argues that clarity in expectations ultimately builds better organizations.
key_points: Flat organizations without titles don't create meritocracy—they create shadow hierarchies that favor insiders and alienate diverse perspectives
True meritocracy requires explicit, clearly articulated expectations for each level, shared transparently with the entire team
If someone can 'lawyerball' their way to promotion using your criteria, either your criteria are incomplete or your bias is blocking deserved recognition
Promotion committees of senior engineers remove manager discretion and create peer-driven accountability for advancement
Meritocracy doesn't mean only rewarding the naturally gifted—it means creating clear paths for people who work hard to grow
money_quotes: Engineers like to believe in the idea of meritocracy. We want to believe that the best idea wins, that the person who produces the most value is rewarded, that we judge only on what a person brings to the table and nothing more.
Removing titles and pretending that the hierarchy doesn't exist does exactly the opposite of creating a meritocracy.
It most often creates a self-reinforcing system where shadow hierarchies rule the day and those outside the in-group have even less opportunity to see their ideas come to life.
'Meritocracy' doesn't mean 'Only reward people who are naturally gifted at what I value.' That's the thing I'm trying to stop doing!
If a person truly is meeting everything I have laid out as being necessary for working at a level, why would I not want to promote them to that level?
Creating a culture where ideas are welcome from anywhere requires cultivation with or without titles.
Promoting someone is not a gift bestowed by management but an acknowledgement by a group of peers that one should join their ranks.
posts.key_insight: Eliminating formal structure doesn't create fairness—it just makes the real power dynamics invisible; true meritocracy requires explicit expectations and transparent processes.

posts.summary: Fournier argues that effective leadership is not about making many decisions, but about making as few decisions as possible while empowering others to decide. She illustrates this through her 2014 decision to push for daily deployments at Rent the Runway, which succeeded because the groundwork was already laid and the team was ready. However, she reveals the real insight: this decision only happened because she had quiet time to think during the holidays. The deeper lesson is that leaders need regular protected time for strategic thinking, which led her to block Wednesday afternoons for reflection.
key_points: Good leadership means making decisions mundane and empowering others to make them without you
Great decisions often aren't revolutionary—they're well-timed pushes that raise the bar when the team is ready to clear it
Leaders need regular solitary time away from meetings to think strategically about the future
Policies exist to make future decisions easier and distribute decision-making authority
The transition from head of engineering to CTO requires shifting focus from day-to-day operations to future-oriented thinking
money_quotes: The role of leadership is in fact to make as few decisions as possible, and to make the decisions that you are forced to make utterly mundane.
The goal of pretty much all of these policies is to make future decisions easier, and to empower various people on my team to make decisions without me.
I want the one ring to release every day. Even if there's no user visible changes. Even if there's traffic. Even if it means we break things.
A great decision is often not a revolutionary move.
All I did was provide the push, to raise the bar just a little bit higher and express my confidence that we would easily clear it.
I need regular time alone, away from meetings and people, in a quiet room with a whiteboard.
I made time for the important but not urgent.
The best decision, really, was to make time to think.
posts.key_insight: The most important leadership decision is creating protected time for strategic thinking, which enables all other good decisions to emerge.

posts.summary: Fournier reflects on her experience attending Spark Camp, a gathering primarily for media and newsroom leaders where she was invited as a tech industry representative. Despite initial impostor syndrome surrounded by creative luminaries, she discovered that management challenges are universal across industries. The post argues that tech workers aren't uniquely difficult to manage—creative professionals in media and arts face identical struggles with talented, option-rich employees. She also notes the shared challenge of leaders feeling creatively stifled by management responsibilities, and concludes with advice on combating impostor syndrome by embracing uncomfortable situations.
key_points: Tech industry leaders mistakenly believe they face unique management challenges with knowledge workers, but media and creative industries deal with identical issues
Talented writers and artists have just as many career options as talented engineers, making retention equally challenging across fields
Creative professionals who become leaders universally struggle to find creative outlets within their management roles
The best antidote to impostor syndrome is saying yes to situations that feel beyond your depth and handling them with grace
Sharing struggles and doubts with strangers from different industries provides valuable perspective on your own challenges
money_quotes: It is valuable to get out of your own head, and see what others are thinking. It is even more valuable to get out of your own industry and see what others are struggling with.
My biggest takeaway was that we in tech think we are dealing with a special situation, a special workforce. We are not alone.
Writers and artists are not all starving, and the talented ones have as many options as talented engineers.
People with backgrounds in the creative arts discover themselves with leadership positions that offer little time, space, or appropriate opportunity for creativity.
Here's my trick: say yes to situations, and try to handle them with grace even when you feel completely out of your depth.
Because under the surface we all struggle, and we all doubt, and sharing those struggles and doubts with strangers is sometimes the best way to free yourself from them.
Vulnerability inspires investment. Management is not performance art.
posts.key_insight: Tech leaders who believe they face unique challenges managing knowledge workers should look outside their industry to discover that creative professionals everywhere struggle with the same management dynamics.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier writes to her team explaining the purpose and value of performance reviews despite their reputation as a flawed process. She emphasizes that reviews serve multiple purposes: celebrating accomplishments, developing self-promotion skills, receiving honest feedback on areas for improvement, reinforcing company values, and setting goals for growth. She encourages team members to take the process seriously both as recipients and as reviewers, noting that some of her most valuable career feedback came through this process. The letter frames reviews as an investment in personal development rather than a bureaucratic exercise.
key_points: Celebrating successes is as valuable as identifying areas for improvement—learning happens when we study both
Self-promotion is a career skill worth developing because not all future managers will track your accomplishments
Written reviews allow reflection on year-long trends and patterns that real-time feedback often misses
Performance reviews reinforce shared company values and remind everyone what the organization stands for
Goal setting during reviews creates structured opportunities to discuss career direction with your manager
money_quotes: There is as much value in knowing what you are good at and what others appreciate in you as there is in knowing what to change.
Self-promotion feels bad to some people, but it is important to learn the skill enough to write about your accomplishments.
You will have many managers throughout your career and they will not always have this ability.
This isn't the time to be petty but it is the time to be thoughtful and honest.
Ask me about the time I got review feedback that told me I was creating a 'culture of fear.'
Learning happens when we study our successes as well as our failures.
The prose is less important than the content, a bulleted list that says something meaningful is fine if that is your style.
posts.key_insight: Performance reviews, despite their flaws, provide unique value by forcing structured reflection on accomplishments, trends in behavior, and career direction that regular feedback cycles rarely capture.

posts.summary: The CTO role is fundamentally a business strategy and management position, not the pinnacle of the technical career ladder. Fournier argues that CTOs must be 'strategic technical executives' who understand the business and can shape strategy through technology, not just the best coders. The role varies widely across companies, but the common thread is combining strategic thinking with executive ability to direct people toward business goals. She warns that CTOs who give up management responsibility lose their power to influence business strategy, becoming figureheads reliant on organizational goodwill. The post challenges the common misconception that CTO is a natural progression for engineers who love deep technical work.
key_points: CTO is not the top of the technical ladder and not necessarily the best engineer in the company
The CTO must be an executive first and technologist second, with a seat at the executive table
CTOs must protect the technology team from becoming a pure execution arm for others' ideas
Giving up management responsibility means giving up power over business strategy
The role definition varies widely but should focus on being the strategic technical executive the company needs
money_quotes: CTO is not an engineering role. CTO is not the top of the technical ladder, it is not the natural progression for engineers over the course of their careers to strive to achieve.
The CTO is an executive first, a technologist second.
The CTO must protect the technology team from becoming a pure execution arm for ideas without tending to its own needs and its own ideas.

If you are a leader with no power over business strategy and no ability to allocate people to important tasks, you are at best at the mercy of your influence with other executives and managers, and at worst a figurehead.
You cannot give up the responsibility of management without giving up the power that comes with it.

If you give up management, you're giving up the most important power you ever had over the business strategy, and you effectively have nothing but your organizational goodwill and your own two hands. Hope you're really a 10X engineer!
posts.key_insight: The CTO role is fundamentally about business strategy and management power, not technical excellence—giving up management responsibility means becoming a figurehead without real influence.

posts.summary: Fournier shares advice that transformed her leadership approach: replacing aggressive questioning with genuine curiosity. She acknowledges her natural instinct to attack and dissect the unknown—useful for debugging systems but damaging in interpersonal contexts. By getting curious instead of defensive, leaders can honor their need for clarity while creating psychological safety. This approach enables learning organizations where people feel safe to fail and new ideas can flourish.
key_points: Aggressive skepticism that serves engineers well in debugging systems can come across as 'obnoxious trolling' in interpersonal interactions
'Get curious' means assuming others have context you're missing rather than assuming they haven't considered your objections
Curiosity creates psychological safety—it enables teams to discuss failures without blame
This approach is a 'bigger picture version' of the improv technique 'Yes, and...' that the author finds easier to maintain
Curiosity is effective even when you need to challenge others—it creates change more effectively than aggression
money_quotes: Whether by virtue of personality or experience, my instincts always lead me to attack and take apart the unknown.
Unfortunately, when it comes to interpersonal interactions, aggressively looking for flaws in things you don't understand looks less like smart defensive behavior and more like obnoxious trolling.
Instead of assuming that the other party hasn't thought of your objections, try to understand deeply the context in which they are making their statements.
The possibility that you're missing something important. And that openness makes people want to work with you.
Curiosity in the face of failure, instead of blame, leads to a safe environment for people to fail, and lets us discuss our failures.
You'll be surprised at how much more effectively you can create change when you approach situations with open curiosity.
posts.key_insight: Replacing aggressive skepticism with genuine curiosity allows leaders to maintain intellectual rigor while creating the psychological safety teams need to take risks and learn from failure.

posts.summary: The post explores the concept of the 'entrepreneurial gap'—giving people more accountability than they have direct resources to accomplish. This approach works brilliantly in startups and growth companies when teams share aligned goals, encouraging cross-functional collaboration and creative problem-solving. However, the same dynamic becomes toxic when organizations have misaligned or competing goals, leading to political maneuvering rather than collaboration. The key insight is that organizational alignment is the prerequisite that makes stretching people beyond their direct control productive rather than destructive.
key_points: The entrepreneurial gap means holding people accountable for outcomes bigger than their direct resources can accomplish
This approach thrives in startups because entrepreneurial employees find ways to gather support and align efforts across the organization
Successful implementations require company-wide alignment on a few clear goals, often centered on the customer
Without alignment, the entrepreneurial gap creates toxic politics as peers compete for scarce resources toward different outcomes
The balance requires both stretching people beyond their control AND ensuring organizational alignment
money_quotes: The idea is straightforward: give people more accountability than they have the direct resources to accomplish.
In many organizations employees are generally held accountable only for things fully within their control.

Because early startup employees tend to be very entrepreneurial they often find a way to do just that, to gather support from other people in the organization and align efforts to produce outsized results.

In companies where everyone is working ultimately towards the same goals, the entrepreneurial gap is awesome because it encourages people to work together and identify opportunities and efficiencies, especially cross-functionally, that may otherwise be lost.

When you are in direct competition with your peers for scarce resources and you are not going to be graded on the same outcomes, the entrepreneurial gap produces a toxic environment of politics and power plays.
Organizational alignment is important because it lets you successfully ask more from people than the resources they have at hand.
Without organizational alignment, you get political maneuvering. Without stretching people beyond their direct control, you get a lack of collaboration and creative cross-functional engagement.
posts.key_insight: Stretching people beyond their direct resources only produces collaboration and innovation when the organization is aligned on shared goals; without that alignment, the same dynamic breeds destructive politics.

posts.summary: New engineering leaders at startups often struggle with recruiting because they lack the institutional support systems they had at larger companies. Facing rejection and pressure to hire, many leaders resort to chasing trendy technologies to attract candidates. However, engineers drawn primarily by 'cool' technology tend to either leave quickly or create unmaintainable solutions. The real interesting problems at early-stage companies are business survival and organizational growth, not technical challenges that require cutting-edge tools. Leaders should instead recruit people who want ownership, care about the business, and seek leadership opportunities.
key_points: Startup recruiting is fundamentally different from recruiting at larger companies with established support structures and competitive salaries
Chasing buzzword technologies to attract candidates often backfires - you attract engineers who won't commit to solving your actual business problems
Early-stage companies face business and organizational challenges, not primarily technical ones - 'cool tech' distracts from real problems
The best recruits are those interested in ownership, the business itself, customers, and leadership opportunities
money_quotes: You were fooled by success gained inside of a company with hidden structures that helped you succeed, the invisible backpack of big(ger) company privilege.
Usually engineers that are only interested in Cool New Technology are not going to stick with your Boring Business Problem for long enough to be worthwhile.
They'll stick with it long enough to leave a trail of one-off solutions that no one else on the team understands before they walk away and leave you holding the bag.
In the early days oversolving with cool tech only leads to distraction from tackling the real challenges.
Don't undersell the opportunity you have just because it isn't Cool New Technology.
Be honest with yourself about the real problems that make you excited about the business that you're in, and that's where you'll find your best sales pitch.
posts.key_insight: The most effective recruiting pitch isn't trendy technology—it's honest enthusiasm about real business problems and opportunities for ownership and growth.

posts.summary: The post distinguishes between external and internal qualities of leadership, arguing that visionary leaders are often evaluated on public-facing traits like charisma, communication, and vision, while the harder-to-assess qualities of listening, being present, staying calm, and building trust are equally essential. Trust is described as a contract built through consistent presence and steady interaction. The author argues that great external leaders need management skills, while managers need leadership qualities like communication, connection, strength, and their own form of vision—seeing what their team can become. The piece encourages finding balance between leading and managing based on situational needs.
key_points: External leadership qualities (charisma, vision, communication) are publicly visible and what boards and VCs invest in, but internal qualities (listening, being there, calm, trust) are equally essential
Trust is a contract constantly negotiated through consistent presence and showing up—it requires the 'relationship qualities' of management
Managers fail without communication, connection, and strength to handle day-to-day challenges like firings, resignations, and missed deadlines
Vision isn't just for strategists—managers need vision too, but theirs focuses on what their team can grow to accomplish over time
Great leaders need management skills; great managers need leadership qualities—the goal is finding the right balance for the situation
money_quotes: Trust is a contract between two people. You are constantly creating and building trust in a long-term relationship with everyone around you.
Without that listening, without 'being there' in a way that lets people feel the ground beneath their feet, without calm, trust is fragile or non-existent.
Trust is regularly tested and negotiated based on our ability to show up.
I would say that these more private qualities, these relationship qualities, are the qualities of management that every great leader must possess.
Managers know the value of steadiness, of showing up for that 1-1 every week, of reacting slowly and listening to the people around them.
A great manager connects with their employees as human beings (without turning into full-time therapist).

The manager's vision sees what their organization looks like in 2 years, what their team can grow to be capable of accomplishing, what the successful day to day looks and feels like for the employees on their team.
posts.key_insight: While visionary leaders are celebrated for their public-facing charisma and strategic vision, sustainable leadership requires the quieter management qualities of consistent presence, active listening, and steadily building trust through showing up.

posts.summary: A manager discovers that a senior engineer has secretly spent two weeks rewriting a core system in an entirely new language instead of adding the requested feature. Fournier argues that while engineer initiative should be encouraged, there's legitimate overhead to adding new technologies that warrants process. She recommends having clear policies for introducing new languages/frameworks, requiring advocacy, learning commitments, and production readiness plans. When violations occur, managers should first understand the engineer's perspective—they may reveal communication failures on both sides. The conversation should then clarify expectations around scope communication and socializing changes with the team.
key_points: Engineer initiative is valuable, but adding new languages/frameworks has real overhead that justifies some process around adoption
A good policy requires advocates to present benefits, create production standards, and have multiple engineers commit to learning the new technology
When someone goes rogue, first ask why—you might discover you're unapproachable or that they feared being shot down
Senior engineers are expected to push information to managers and socialize changes with their team, not work in isolation
Part of seniority is knowing when scope changes require communication, not just technical competence
money_quotes: The last thing you want to do is stifle people's initiative to create solid solutions and learn new things.
There is a high overhead to adding new things to your stack, and at some point, it usually makes sense to have some policy around how to add new things.
Part of being a senior engineer is knowing when to communicate scope changes such as the need to move to new languages or frameworks.

Perhaps you skip all of their 1-1s and they think you are unapproachable. Perhaps they are frustrated with the way you make decisions. Perhaps they knew you would be mad and were simply afraid to show you new work early when you would shoot it down.
Socializing change not only to your manager but to your team is part of the role of the senior engineer.
All too often teams will reject projects they weren't aware of and didn't have any say in.
posts.key_insight: When an engineer goes rogue, the problem is rarely just about the engineer—investigate whether your own approachability or decision-making created conditions where they felt hiding work was the safer path.

posts.summary: The author argues that product and feature decisions should be guided by explicit theories rather than relying solely on data-driven experimentation or vague intuitions. She points out that complex multivariate environments make it impossible to test all permutations of changes, so teams must be thoughtful about their choices. Having a clear theory means understanding the chain of cause and effect you expect, especially for features targeting second or third-order impacts. Without a theory, you leave yourself vulnerable to unfalsifiable outcomes like 'you just didn't implement it well enough.'
key_points: Data and experimentation alone cannot guide decisions in complex multivariate environments where testing all permutations is impossible
Features targeting indirect benefits (loyalty, brand perception) require a clear theory of the causal chain leading to those outcomes
You must measure the immediate expected effects of your theory, not just hope existing metrics improve
Without a theory, you cannot distinguish between a bad idea and poor implementation
Being 'data-driven' is often more rhetoric than reality—choices still require thoughtfulness
money_quotes: In this day and age we sometimes get lazy about thoughtfulness, and rely on data and experimentation to hill-climb our way through the world around us.
The reality is that we're working in such complex multivariate environments that we cannot possibly test all permutations of even the simplest change.

The only way that we can make sane choices in a complex world is by actually being thoughtful about the choices we are making, creating a theory, and creating experiments that actually test that theory.
If you're going to try to get second or third order effects from a feature, you'd better have a really solid theory of the chain of events that leads to those second or third order impacts.
Don't just look at the number of people buying the product and hope it goes up.
The worst of these is the 'you just didn't implement it well enough' outcome.
It's impossible to prove or disprove without anticipating the question ahead of time, thinking through the logical conclusions of the theory, and setting up a good test to understand its outcome.
posts.key_insight: True data-driven decision making requires having an explicit theory about cause and effect before you experiment, otherwise you're just measuring and hoping.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier offers hard-won lessons for engineering managers transitioning to startups, drawing parallels to distributed systems challenges. She emphasizes that startup management lacks the invisible support systems of big companies—recruiting pipelines, established processes, HR infrastructure—forcing managers to build their own safety nets. The post covers coordination difficulties, trust-building, capacity estimation, feedback loops, organizational structure, and the inevitability of people quitting. Her core message is that the social challenges of management far exceed the technical ones, and survival requires building scalable processes while maintaining genuine connection with your team.
key_points: Big company managers don't realize how many invisible systems support them until they're gone; startup managers must build their own safety net of processes and standards
Coordination overhead is so massive that the industry invented microservices rather than force teams to work together effectively
As a leader, you must figure out how to get information about bottlenecks and problems without actually doing the work yourself—that IS the job
Engineers want to ship, and if they're not shipping, they'll either overengineer systems, quit, or both
People will inevitably quit regardless of how good a boss you are; your job is to identify systemic causes you can fix and keep going
money_quotes: You don't see the millions of invisible systems all around you that have set you up for success.

The amount of overhead that goes into managing coordination of people cannot be overstated. It's so great that the industry invented microservices because we'd rather invest engineering headcount and dollars into software orchestration than force disparate engineering teams to work together.
'The team is moving too slow' is the hardest problem you'll ever debug.
You must figure out how to get information about the bottlenecks and problems in the development process without actually having to do it all yourself. That is the job.
You may not personally be creating technical debt much anymore, but that doesn't mean you aren't creating organizational debt.
Engineers want to ship. This goes double for startup engineers. If they are not shipping much, they will start to complain of boredom, and go looking for ways to make trouble.
If you're bored, try doing it all 20% cheaper.
If you care about your team, helping them move on when they want to move on is a great honor.
posts.key_insight: Startup engineering management requires building the invisible support systems that big companies provide while accepting that coordination, feedback loops, and people leaving are fundamentally hard problems you'll never fully solve.

posts.summary: The post examines Daniel Pink's popular framework of Autonomy, Mastery, and Purpose as intrinsic motivators, arguing that it's incomplete for understanding workplace motivation. Fournier draws on her own experience in grad school, where she had autonomy and purpose but felt lost and unsupported, to illustrate the gap. She synthesizes insights from multiple books on motivation to identify a fourth crucial element: Relatedness—the need to feel known and connected to coworkers. She argues that while engineers tend to focus on the first three motivators, Relatedness is often what's missing and is actually the easiest for managers to provide.
key_points: Daniel Pink's Autonomy, Mastery, Purpose framework resonates with engineers but is incomplete
The author's negative grad school experience—despite having autonomy and purpose—revealed that something was missing from the framework
Relatedness (feeling known and connected at work) is the fourth intrinsic motivator often overlooked in tech
Engineers tend to dismiss Relatedness because it's 'touchy-feely' and hard to quantify
Relatedness is paradoxically the easiest motivator for managers to provide—it just requires genuine human interest
money_quotes: Anyone who has ever managed teams has probably spent some time being alternately perplexed and frustrated with the difficulty of motivating people, especially engineers.
I had no idea where to go, no idea what to do, and felt like my work had no point and moreover I had no one to help me.
Relatedness is the fourth element that is absent from Drive.

It does not surprise me that engineers would cling to the first three motivators without thinking of the fourth. Relatedness is very touchy-feely. It's hard to quantify. It requires interpersonal engagement.
As a leader, you will lead people who want Relatedness in their job. They want you to know about their family, their hobbies.
The nice thing about Relatedness is that it is the easiest thing to provide.
You may find that once you start caring about people, you feel a bit happier yourself at work.
