posts.key_insight: The popular Autonomy-Mastery-Purpose framework for motivation overlooks Relatedness—feeling known and connected at work—which is both commonly missing in engineering cultures and the easiest element for managers to provide.

posts.summary: Fournier reflects on why her publicly-released Rent the Runway engineering ladder became so widely adopted across the tech industry. She argues that the timing coincided with a cultural shift away from flat, process-free organizations as companies realized those structures often amplified the loudest voices rather than creating equality. The detailed nature of the ladder filled a gap—providing a thoughtful starting point is more valuable than a basic outline that companies must flesh out themselves. She advocates for 'open source culture' where organizations share not just code but HR documents, policies, and cultural artifacts to improve the entire industry.
key_points: Flat organizations didn't deliver on their promise—having no titles often meant only the loudest voices were heard, not that all voices were equal
Detailed career ladders help combat unconscious bias by providing clarity on advancement, benefiting everyone not just those most affected by bias
Public ladders serve as powerful recruiting tools by showing candidates what they're getting into and what career paths exist
Sharing organizational documents accelerates adoption because teams are more comfortable with known, widely-adopted frameworks
The secrecy around HR documents is an unquestioned norm worth challenging, just as we've normalized sharing source code
money_quotes: Suddenly we saw that having no title didn't actually mean that every voice in the organization was heard equally, it often just meant that the loudest voices were the only voices heard at all.
Just as you only use the features of open source software that you actually need, you only need to take the details from open source culture that you want.
People are getting used to the idea of an engineering ladder as a tool to help them progress in their career, not a tool to stifle them or create unnecessary hierarchy.
The veil of secrecy around HR-related issues has existed without questioning for a long time. Let's question it!
You can't open source what you haven't created or modified.

We're all comfortable with sharing our source code, and we might blog or speak about the processes for running our teams, but few of us have ever shared the core documents for running our organizations.
posts.key_insight: Organizations should share their cultural documents and HR policies as openly as they share code, because detailed, public frameworks help combat bias, improve hiring, and accelerate healthy culture-building across the industry.

posts.summary: Fournier examines why the technical track exists and its inherent limitations compared to management. She argues that she chose management because her ambitions exceeded what she could accomplish independently through code alone. The piece challenges the notion that technical specialists and managers can be equal partners, since management authority over people inevitably translates to decision-making power. She contends that reluctant managers signal a broken culture that overvalues coding, and that the technical track primarily exists to retain valuable contributors whose impact may not match that of managers.
key_points: Individual contributors are fundamentally limited by resources they don't control, regardless of technical skill
The person with hiring/firing authority will always have influence over what gets done, making true manager-specialist partnerships unrealistic
A culture of reluctant managers indicates coding is overvalued relative to management, which is a dysfunction
Senior technical staff trade career clarity and outsized impact for more intellectual freedom and less time pressure
Expecting faster technical track promotions misses that most companies don't need many people at senior IC levels
money_quotes: I realized pretty clearly that the scope of my ambitions was greater than the scope of my ability to produce things independently.
It is unrealistic to believe that the person with management authority over the people would have no influence over what they do, even with best intentions assumed.
Reluctant management is a sign of a broken system, not a necessary an expected outcome of a growing engineering organization.

The technical track exists so that we won't lose people who do good work and have valuable institutional knowledge, but the impact that those people have is often not equivalent to the impact that managers have.
There are diminishing returns for people who are possibly just incrementally better at building software.

The superpower of a senior technical staffer is that they get to work on harder intellectual problems and/or more speculative work, they get the luxury of a calendar that is far freer of obligatory meetings.
If you're not a little uncomfortable, what makes you think you're really growing into a new role?
posts.key_insight: Management authority over people inherently translates to decision-making power, making the technical track a trade of impact and career clarity for intellectual freedom and schedule control.

posts.summary: The post draws a parallel between debugging complex software systems and diagnosing team dysfunction, arguing that the best engineering managers excel at both because they share the same relentless pursuit of root causes. Fournier explains that teams, like systems, are black boxes with observable inputs and outputs but hidden internal workings, and that observation itself can change behavior (like adding log statements hiding concurrency bugs). She walks through a detailed example of debugging a 'slow' team, examining records, meetings, goals, and team dynamics to find underlying issues. The core argument is that managers must apply the same systematic rigor to organizational problems as they would to production incidents, rather than relying on charm or luck.
key_points: Great debuggers and great managers share a relentless drive to understand 'why' rather than accepting surface-level explanations
Teams are black boxes where observation changes behavior, similar to how debugging can mask concurrency issues
Diagnosing team problems requires examining multiple layers: records, meetings, goals clarity, and interpersonal dynamics
Boring meetings, unclear goals, and lack of team connection are key indicators of deeper organizational dysfunction
Senior leaders should help debug team issues rather than simply holding managers accountable for outputs
money_quotes: Managing teams is a series of complex, black boxes interacting with other complex, black boxes.
Your presence changes the team behavior and may hide the problem you are trying to find, in the same way that a log statement can cause a concurrency issue to be magically erased.
Boring meetings are a sure sign of time wasted, if not bigger leadership problems.
If they don't understand the goals of their work, their leaders are not doing a good job engaging the team in the purpose of the work.
A bunch of people who never talk to each other and are always working on independent projects are not really working as a team.
Without the drive to understand why, we rely on charm and luck to see us through our management career.
The pursuit of why when it comes to organizational problems is the thing that gives you patterns to match on, and lessons to lead with.
posts.key_insight: Effective management requires the same systematic, hypothesis-driven approach to diagnosing team dysfunction that great engineers apply to debugging complex systems.

posts.summary: Reorganizations are a necessary evil in companies of all sizes, functioning as a 'full GC' of organizational structure. The author outlines a framework for successful reorgs based on five elements: vision (the why), skills (especially communication), incentives (buy-in from the team), resources (time, focus, and adequate staffing), and action plan (clear execution steps). Drawing from three reorgs over four years, she emphasizes that leaders must understand and communicate the purpose clearly, advocate for their team's interests, avoid stretching resources too thin, and accept that perfection is impossible. Reorgs won't solve cultural issues or replace business strategy, but when done thoughtfully with peer collaboration, they can move companies toward healthier, more productive structures.
key_points: Reorgs are often necessary but should not be undertaken lightly - they are major disruptions that require clear business justification, not just organizational tinkering
Communication is the most critical skill for leaders during reorgs; poor timing and inconsistent messaging create rumor mills and spike anxiety
Resource constraints must be respected - creating one more team than you can adequately staff will lead to failure and demoralize people given then removed from leadership opportunities
Teams with conflicting goals will create turf wars; analytical scrutiny of goal alignment is essential before finalizing structure
False starts from inadequate planning create prolonged uncertainty that damages team morale and productivity
money_quotes: Reorgs are disruptive and stressful, but I personally think they are often a necessary evil, the 'full GC' of organizational structure change.

When reorgs go bad, one of the ways they go bad is that the reason for the change is not communicated well to the whole team, and the timing of communication is not coordinated, so some people know what's happening, others don't, and thus starts the rumor mill and the anxiety skyrockets.

If you put people into leadership opportunities and then take them away, even if it is a good business decision and not an indictment of the person, they will still see it as a loss and you are very likely to lose them over it.
It's a cliché that every 'Yes' you say implies a 'no' to something else, but saying 'no' to stretching your team too thin in a reorg is an essential part of making it successful.
If you have multiple teams with conflicting goals, you will quite possibly end up with turf wars.
Reorgs are unlikely to solve all of your problems overnight. They are not a magic bullet exercise that fixes cultural issues or a lack of business strategy.
If you aren't careful, your reorg will feel like rearranging deck chairs on the Titanic, a pointless waste of time for everyone involved.
posts.key_insight: Successful reorganizations require treating them as serious operational undertakings with clear vision, coordinated communication, realistic resource constraints, and aligned goals - not as quick fixes for deeper organizational problems.

posts.summary: Fournier argues that being a 'technology company' isn't about producing software, but about embracing holistic rather than prescriptive approaches to work. Drawing on Ursula Franklin's framework, she distinguishes between assembly-line processes where workers execute discrete tasks versus holistic practices where individuals or teams control the entire process. She connects this to common tech company values like transparency, learning, and embracing change, arguing these naturally emerge when you value holistic technology. Companies that claim technology as merely a competitive advantage without embracing these cultural values are not true tech companies.
key_points: The production of software is insufficient for a company to hold 'technology' as a core cultural value - what matters is whether they value holistic versus prescriptive approaches to work
Holistic technology practices give workers control over the full process, while prescriptive practices break work into standardized steps that enable managerial control and surveillance
Tech company values like transparency, learning, and embracing change are natural consequences of valuing holistic technology work
Companies that view technology only as competitive advantage or intellectual property moat, without embracing engineering culture values, are not true technology companies
Early startups are inherently holistic due to small size, but the test comes as they grow and face pressure to become prescriptive
money_quotes: The production of software is certainly insufficient for a company to hold 'technology' as a core cultural value.
Software has so far rebuffed efforts to fully turn it into a prescriptive style of work over long periods of time.

To value it as a competitive advantage, intellectual property that forms a moat around your business, without valuing the common shared cultural values of engineers themselves, is insincere and hollow.
This is the value of someone who would happily turn their engineering team into robots who produce exactly what they are asked to produce, when asked.
In complex software, it is likely that no individual understands the whole, but that the group must get together and act holistically to address and solve problems.
A few people can also negatively impact huge numbers of people by making a mistake, releasing a bad feature, taking down a system.
posts.key_insight: A true technology company values holistic work practices that empower teams to see and solve problems end-to-end, not just the intellectual property that technology produces.

posts.summary: When hiring engineering managers, organizations must be thoughtful about whether they're screening for management potential or prior experience. For potential-driven hires, look beyond technical skills to evidence of stepping up, caring about team members, and improving processes. These candidates should demonstrate project management involvement beyond just technical contributions, active engagement in onboarding and mentoring, and initiative in solving organizational problems. However, hiring for potential requires commitment to training and mentorship, as no one is born knowing how to manage.
key_points: Hiring people who want to eventually move into leadership but are comfortable starting as ICs is essential for growing management from within
Screening for potential is fundamentally different from screening for experience and requires different interview approaches
Look for candidates who did more than just technical work as tech leads - they should have contributed to project management, predicted delivery problems, and worked cross-functionally
Be aware of bias when evaluating leadership potential - the best managers often don't pattern match to stereotypes like 'tall and male' or 'forceful personality'
If you hire someone for potential, be prepared to train them - they will struggle if they have to do it alone
money_quotes: If you want to grow management from within but don't bother to look for people who want to grow into management roles when you're hiring, you may end up with mostly people who just want to code, and that is not good for anyone.
Screening for potential is different than screening for experience.
Someone who respected the work of mentoring, who isn't just trying to shed human interactions quickly to get back to code.
The best managers are often overlooked because they don't pattern match to certain characteristics, whether they are 'tall and male' or 'forceful personality' or whatever.

When you mishire on potential, it tends to come from overvaluing technical skills + pattern matching on 'what a leader looks like', and the failure mode is managers who just don't deliver coherent teams and/or can't deliver projects effectively.
Never ever hire a person you feel is overly ego-driven.
No one is born knowing how to manage.
posts.key_insight: Successfully hiring engineering managers for potential requires looking beyond technical excellence to find evidence of stepping up, caring about people, and process improvement - while actively guarding against leadership stereotype bias.

posts.summary: The post argues that even when data isn't available for decision-making, teams should maintain an analytical mindset through qualitative methods. Drawing from an Etsy engineering leadership panel, Fournier synthesizes perspectives on balancing data-driven decisions with vision-driven product development. She contends that new products lack the primary data needed for quantitative analysis, but this doesn't mean abandoning rigor. Instead, teams should form clear hypotheses about customers, articulate why they'd use the product, and define what success signals to watch for. The key insight is that qualitative research—user testing, empathy, observation—is still analytical work, not just guessing.
key_points: Even vision-driven projects should connect to measurable goals and articulate how they contribute to larger objectives
Qualitative methods like user research and observation are essential for product-market fit, not just number optimization
New products lack primary user data, making quantitative A/B testing impractical until significant scale is reached
Building products requires clear hypotheses about who the customer is, why they'd use it, and what engagement signals to measure
Customer empathy and observation can drive better products faster than relying on numbers alone
money_quotes: Qualitative is still analytical.

It is hard to do quantitative analysis without data, and new things only have secondary data about potential and markets, they do not have primary data about the actual user engagement with the unbuilt product that you can measure.
Instinct and guesses are necessary. But we needn't lose our analytical approach just because we don't have data.
When you build something, you have a hypothesis about the person you are building it for.
Answering the questions of who is my customer, why would she use this, and what will signs of interest and engagement look like is essential to going from vague instinct to thoughtful first product.
Data can't make all our decisions for us because data isn't there to get us from 0->1.
Customer empathy is likely to drive us more quickly to great products than simply relying on numbers alone.
posts.key_insight: Qualitative methods are not the absence of rigor—they're analytical approaches that use hypothesis-driven thinking and customer empathy when quantitative data doesn't yet exist.

posts.summary: New managers often struggle when they transition from fighting fires to having unstructured time with no clear urgent tasks. The post compares management to operations work, where practitioners get good at handling ad hoc needs and emergencies but neglect long-term strategic thinking. Fournier advises new managers to use downtime productively by learning existing systems, building relationships with other teams, and doing strategic planning for hiring, reviews, and team improvements. Crucially, she warns against the temptation to fill empty time by creating unnecessary work for others or interrupting busy engineers. The post emphasizes that managers must learn to define what work needs to be done rather than waiting to be told, while also recognizing that sometimes the best use of downtime is simply taking a breath.
key_points: Management downtime parallels operations work: managers get good at fires and ad hoc tasks but struggle with long-term strategic projects
New managers should use downtime to learn systems, meet other teams, and understand the broader organizational landscape
Strategic thinking during downtime should focus on important-but-not-urgent work like planning reviews, proactive hiring outreach, and addressing recurring team problems
Managers must resist filling empty time by meddling, micromanaging, or creating half-baked work for others to support
Part of a manager's job is defining what work needs to be done, not waiting for their boss to tell them
money_quotes: It's much easier to do things right in front of you than it is to think abstractly about unclear topics.
Remember, part of your job now is to define what work needs to be done. Your boss won't always be able to tell you what to do, and she probably expects you to tell her what needs to be done.
If you use your downtime to wander around and interrupt your engineers who are busy working, that is harmful (and yes, I'm guilty of that sometimes!).
You are responsible for finding productive uses of downtime, and part of that is resisting the urge to meddle, micromanage, and distract your team just because you don't know what else to do.
There are times when the best thing you can do with downtime is take a breath, check in on the internet, watch a talk you have been meaning to see.
Don't worry, there will be another meeting, another fire soon enough. Enjoy it while it lasts.
posts.key_insight: A manager's job includes defining what work needs to be done, and learning to use unstructured time for strategic thinking rather than filling it with busywork or micromanagement is a critical skill.

posts.summary: The author describes her evolution from skeptic to advocate of explicit company values, based on her experience at Rent The Runway. Through observing performance reviews and 'core value stories,' she noticed that the most beloved, happy, and productive employees weren't necessarily the on-paper superstars, but those whose personal values naturally aligned with company values. She argues that explicit values provide a more equitable framework for evaluating culture fit than vague notions that can become proxies for demographic similarity. The post concludes with practical advice for incorporating values into the interview process.
key_points: Explicit company values can reduce bias by focusing on behavior and communication rather than demographic characteristics
The most successful employees are often those whose personal values naturally align with company values, not necessarily those with the best credentials
Asking about 'culture fit' without specificity invites interviewers to default to bias ('people who look like me')
Values should be made explicit in the interview process by listing specific values and asking interviewers to evaluate against them
Company culture is often confused with demographics, but well-defined values can separate the two
money_quotes: By now, we know that beer and ping pong tables aren't culture.
Many of us fear that 'culture' can be a dog whistle for 'people who look like me.'
Their only common thread was that they didn't have to stretch too much to live the company values, because the company values overlapped with their own personal values.
The way you frame the question of culture is important, and if you aren't explicit, people may skip over the details and go with their bias.

When company values are more explicit, however, they give you something that is (hopefully) less correlated with how people look and more correlated with how people communicate, make decisions, and behave.
They may not have been the people who went to the best schools, or who wrote the most beautiful code, in fact they often weren't the 'on-paper' superstars.
posts.key_insight: Explicit company values, when properly framed in interviews and reviews, can transform 'culture fit' from a bias-prone gut check into an equitable framework for finding employees who will genuinely thrive.

posts.summary: Fournier defends reading 'repetitive' business books like Carol Dweck's Mindset, arguing that while the core concepts can be summarized quickly, deep internalization requires prolonged exposure. She acknowledges the book is repetitive but contends that understanding a concept intellectually is vastly different from putting it into practice. Drawing parallels to Buddhist philosophy and spiritual practice, she suggests that leadership and personal growth concepts require the same lifetime of study. The repetition serves a purpose: spending five hours with an idea rather than fifteen minutes helps it actually sink in and change behavior.
key_points: There's a dangerous fallacy in believing that being able to explain a concept means you understand it well enough to apply it
The gap between understanding a concept and putting it into practice is enormous—similar to knowing diet and exercise help weight loss but struggling to actually do it
Many business and leadership books are essentially rediscoveries of Buddhist principles about letting go of fixed notions of self and embracing change
Prolonged exposure to ideas through a full book helps internalize concepts in ways that short articles cannot
Moving from a fixed to growth mindset is a gradual, ongoing process that requires continuous work
money_quotes: I think that sometimes I fall victim to this fallacy that if I am familiar with the general outline of a concept, and I agree with the concept, that I understand this concept.
There is a huge separation between understanding a concept and putting it into action.
Changing the way you react to things is hard.
It is no surprise that it takes a lifetime of study. So, too, does learning the concepts of leadership, creativity, interacting with fellow humans, and growth.
It helps to get a prolonged exposure to the idea, to work through some of its nuance, to spend 5 hours with it instead of just 15 minutes.
Why am I moving? Mostly because it's just more fun to grow.
I kept meeting these people that seemed so light, so free, so creative, and they just seemed so willing to learn and fail and learn some more. Why not me? Why not all of us?
posts.key_insight: Knowing a concept intellectually is not the same as internalizing it enough to change your behavior—repetitive books serve the vital function of helping ideas actually sink in.

posts.summary: Fournier addresses a manager dealing with an employee who publicly vented anger on team chat after feeling excluded from a brainstorming meeting. She advises having a face-to-face conversation where the manager listens completely without interruption or filling in gaps, then repeats back concerns before offering their own perspective. The angry outburst still requires an apology, but the deeper issue is investigating why trust has broken down. She notes that anger rarely stems from single incidents but from accumulated resentment, making this a warning signal about team dynamics.
key_points: Immediate feedback on inappropriate behavior is correct, even if it escalates - don't let issues linger for days
The manager's primary job in a conflict conversation is to listen completely, hold silence, and avoid telling stories about the other person's motivations
Sustained anger signals accumulated resentment and broken trust, not just reaction to a single incident
As teams grow, not everyone can be in every meeting - develop strategies that let people shape ideas even if they miss inception
When triangulating with others' observations, be careful not to lead them to confirm your preferred narrative
money_quotes: For many people, just listening will be the hardest part. But you gotta get comfortable holding silence even when you're bursting to comment.
No filling in gaps. No telling stories. No imagining motivations beyond what he has told you. Give him the chance to feel totally heard.
People rarely get angry and stay angry after a single minor incident. They get angry and stay angry because they've built up resentment about something over time.
This is a strong warning signal to you that something is broken in your team.
Remind everyone that ideas are just the barest first step. The path from 'idea' to 'implementation' usually twists the idea in ways you can't foresee.
Even if they agree with your assessment that Dan was out of line or tell you that you did nothing wrong, don't use it as a cudgel to bash Dan with.
Dealing with an angry team member is always touchy, but as with most difficulty situations, it gives you the opportunity to look at a bigger picture that you might have been missing.
posts.key_insight: An angry employee is rarely upset about just one incident - their reaction is a warning signal that trust has eroded over time, and your job is to listen without defensiveness to understand the real problem.

posts.summary: The post explores why giving employees books to read rarely produces the behavioral change managers hope for. Fournier argues that people must genuinely want to change before any external resources can help, and that even then, personal change takes significant time. More importantly, she challenges managers to examine whether the person actually needs to change, or whether the environment causing the problematic behavior is what should be addressed. She concludes that changing circumstances is often more effective than trying to change people's reactions to difficult situations.
key_points: Books and other resources only help people who are already open to change and see a need for it
Before trying to change someone's behavior, examine whether the environment or situation is actually the root cause
Personal change is slow and difficult even when people genuinely want to improve
The most successful book recommendations come when people have control over their environment and the book helps them drive change
Reducing stress and improving circumstances is often more achievable than coaching someone to react differently to stress
money_quotes: You can't force people to want to change. You can give them information, books, classes, coaching, but if they don't see a need to change, none of that will help.
Sometimes, we want people to change when in fact what needs to change is the situation we've put them in.
Before you look to change the people, make sure that the change that needs to happen isn't in fact something in the environment that you can control.
Most people are going to behave in unhealthy ways when they are in unhealthy environments.
It is usually easier for a manager to change the environment than it is to change a person's reaction to that environment.
It is pretty rare that I've given an unhappy person a book for reacting better to unhappy circumstances and seen them embrace it.
It is unlikely that you'll coach your team to Buddha nature but you might be able to reduce the stress that is causing the problems in the first place.
posts.key_insight: When an employee exhibits problematic behavior, a manager's first instinct should be to examine the environment causing that behavior rather than trying to fix the person.

posts.summary: A new manager struggling to balance coding with management responsibilities receives advice that reframes the problem: management IS the real work now, not a burden preventing them from coding. The post argues that managers who don't value management as legitimate work cause damage to their teams and themselves. Great managers create value by building functioning teams focused on the right work, which typically exceeds the value they'd create as individual contributors. The transition from hands-on to hands-off is difficult because the feedback loops are much slower and less tangible than coding.
key_points: Management must be viewed as real, valuable work - not an unfortunate burden preventing you from coding
If your team isn't getting enough work done, your job is to help them get more done, not to do the work yourself
Great managers create more organizational value through building functioning teams than they would as even a '10X engineer'
The feedback loop for management is much slower than engineering - there's no 'red-green-refactor for guiding humans'
It's okay to mourn the loss of the quick wins and endorphin hits that come from hands-on technical work
money_quotes: Do you believe that management is real work? Do you believe it is work that is just as valuable as coding, architecture, and other hands-on technical tasks?
If you do not think that management is real work, you will continue to treat it like an unfortunate burden to be dealt with so you can get to the fun stuff.
Your team needs you to be there as a manager for them, and it is almost impossible to show up fully for a role that you don't think is valuable.
Great managers know that their value comes from the ways in which they create functioning teams, focused on the right work.
If your team isn't getting enough work done, your job is not to do the work yourself. Your job as a manager is to figure out how to help the team get more done.
There's no red-green-refactor for guiding humans.
You spent years getting good at the job of being an engineer, and now you have to do something that you probably aren't as good at yet.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental mindset shift for new managers is accepting that management is the real work now, not an obstacle preventing you from doing 'real' technical work.

posts.summary: This post addresses a common management challenge: how to stay informed about team progress without constantly interrupting engineers with status requests. Fournier argues that managers must first learn to read existing project management tools and dashboards before asking people directly. She breaks down different scenarios requiring status updates—boss queries, urgent production issues, and casual curiosity—each demanding different approaches. The key is to respect engineers' focus time, defer to immediate managers and tech leads, and use direct interactions sparingly as opportunities to develop team leadership rather than undermine it.
key_points: Managers should learn to read project dashboards and tracking tools before interrupting engineers with status questions
Different urgency levels require different approaches: routine boss questions can wait, production issues may require immediate developer input
Direct interactions with engineers should be used sparingly and as teaching opportunities to develop team leadership
'Swoop and poop management'—dropping suggestions without context and leaving—is destructive and distracting
The tech lead or manager's job is specifically to manage and communicate project status so engineers can focus
money_quotes: When you are embedded in the team you are leading, the details of their projects are the air you breathe. But add on more teams or a level of management and all of those details provide hard to discover.
You owe it to your team to look at the existing tracking software and look for the status there first, before firing off questions directly to the engineers.
Usually when the boss asks us something, that makes us want to jump quickly. What we really need to do is just make a note to get more information and then get it when the time is right.
The worst outcome is having your boss go directly to the team themselves and ask for a status update.
Asking what they intend to do puts the ball in their court, and forces them to practice taking leadership of the situation.
Swooping down, pooping out an idea or comment, and flying away without seeing the effect of the interaction is, at best, a distraction or annoyance.
It is the job of the tech lead or manager to manage and communicate project status specifically so that the engineers can focus.
posts.key_insight: Effective managers gather status through systems and processes rather than interruptions, reserving direct engineer interactions for urgent situations and leadership development opportunities.

posts.summary: The post examines take-home coding interviews as an alternative to whiteboard coding, comparing Slack's creative project approach with Foursquare's more constrained exercise format. While take-home exercises can reduce bias through blind evaluation, the author argues that creative, open-ended assignments may inadvertently favor candidates with more free time and those who enjoy tinkering. The author personally prefers Foursquare's clinical, time-bounded approach that respects candidates' time while still evaluating technical skills. The piece concludes that companies should thoughtfully design their take-homes to decide whether they're testing technical skills, creativity, or both, and consider offering different formats for different candidates.
key_points: Take-home exercises can serve as the 'blind audition' tech needs to reduce interviewer bias, but must be designed thoughtfully
Asking for 2-4 hours is reasonable if the exercise genuinely fits that timeframe and reduces onsite interview burden
Creative, open-ended take-homes risk biasing toward people with free time who will over-invest, disadvantaging those with caregiving or other responsibilities
Constrained, clinical exercises like Foursquare's respect candidate time while still evaluating technical competency
Different interview formats select for different types of people, so companies should be intentional about what they're optimizing for
money_quotes: White board coding interviews are clearly a strange way to measure a person's ability to actually do the day to day work of a modern software engineer.
They have the potential to be the blind audition step that the tech industry needs.
Just because the exercise says take home doesn't mean you actually have to do it in your off hours.
At this point you're talking about something that is both a test of your programming abilities but also a creative project.
I don't like to tinker, and I prefer that my creative work be collaborative. It feels like you are wasting my time and instead of making me more excited, it makes me far less excited.
Building something that rewards those who can spend far longer is likely to bias against those who have, say, kids to take care of after work and on weekends.
No one ever said that fixing hiring was going to be easy.
posts.key_insight: Well-intentioned take-home interviews can inadvertently introduce new biases by rewarding candidates with more free time, so companies must carefully decide whether they're testing technical skills, creativity, or both.

posts.summary: Fournier argues that laziness and impatience, traditionally seen as vices, are actually virtues for engineering leaders when directed at processes rather than people. She distinguishes between working more hours and actually working faster, emphasizing that 'faster' should mean delivering the same value in less total time, not cramming more hours into fewer days. The post advocates for managers to model healthy work boundaries by going home on time and not sending off-hours emails, as teams mirror their leaders' behavior. Burnout is a real threat that harms individuals, families, and team effectiveness. Leaders should constantly question inefficient processes and strip projects down to their essential value. The virtuous cycle is clear: focus enables going home, and going home forces continued focus.
key_points: Impatience directed at individuals is rude, but impatience directed at processes and decisions drives focus and efficiency
Working 60 hours in one week instead of normal hours over 1.5 weeks isn't 'faster'—it's just giving the company your free time
Leaders must model work-life boundaries because teams will mirror their behavior, even if told they don't have to
The antidote to brute-force problem solving is asking what's truly important and whether value can be delivered more simply
Burnout from sustained overwork is terrible for individuals, families, and teams—and makes engineers less effective at detailed knowledge work
money_quotes: Impatience and laziness, applied to process, are the key elements to focus.
I can't stand watching people waste their energy approaching problems with brute force and spending time rather than thought.
If the team works 60 hours in a week to deliver something that otherwise would've taken a week and a half, they haven't worked faster, they've just given the company more of their free time.

When you work later than everyone else, when you send those emails at all hours, even if you don't expect your team to respond to those emails or work those hours, they see you doing it, and think it's important.
Go home! And stop emailing people at all hours of the night and all hours of the weekend!
We focus so we can go home, and we encourage going home because it forces us to constantly focus. This is how great teams scale.
posts.key_insight: True productivity comes from ruthlessly focusing on what matters and modeling sustainable work habits, not from working longer hours—because teams will always mirror their leaders' behavior.

posts.summary: Fournier argues that hubris, the third programming virtue, extends beyond taking pride in good work to include the willingness to voice complaints and criticism. She challenges the common management practice of 'bring solutions, not complaints,' arguing it suppresses vital context about problems. Complaints contain essential details that help teams understand their specific context, which is crucial for applying appropriate solutions rather than cargo-culting what worked elsewhere. Leaders should guide people to articulate their complaints with specificity, using these discussions as teaching moments about which problems matter. Rather than pursuing false harmony, teams should embrace constructive conflict as a path to genuine improvement.
key_points: Hubris balances impatience by preventing corner-cutting through pride in quality work
Complaints contain essential context details that get lost when only positive sharing is encouraged
'Bring solutions, not complaints' is unrealistic and causes real issues to go unaddressed
Context determines whether any solution is appropriate - microservices, hiring practices, etc.
Leaders should ask clarifying questions about complaints to help teams learn which problems are worth solving
money_quotes: If you squash people who want to complain or criticize, you lose the details of your problems. Those complaints contain the details!
Does your company have a practice of telling people to 'bring solutions, not complaints?' That is at best hiding problems, not avoiding them.
It is unrealistic to expect people to be able to solve every problem they see in front of them.
Applying someone else's lessons to your context without understanding is how we end up with these cargo cult solutions.
Letting people complain might seem like it will do nothing but encourage negativity and drama, but if you guide people to learn from their complaints it can instead help your team grow.
Taking pride sometimes means speaking up when something doesn't seem to be right, when something seems to be less than what it could be.
Trying to create conflict-free environments is a fool's errand.
posts.key_insight: Complaints are not obstacles to team harmony but valuable sources of contextual information that leaders should mine for details rather than suppress in the name of positivity.

posts.summary: Fournier dissects the 'folk architecture' of microservices by examining two distinct use cases: CRUD-based applications evolved from monoliths and stream-processing systems. She argues that microservices emerged from the confluence of scaling woes, cloud accessibility, and comfort with distributed systems. For CRUD applications, she advocates a pragmatic approach—extracting services entity by entity without over-engineering, and questions many 'must-haves' like dynamic service discovery and separate data stores for smaller teams. For stream-centric applications with high data volumes, she acknowledges that investment in service orchestration tooling becomes essential. The core message is that microservices patterns should match actual business needs rather than following prescriptive rules.
key_points: Microservices evolved from three factors: startups hitting monolith scaling walls, cloud making servers accessible, and increased comfort with distributed systems and network calls
The benefits of microservices (independent scaling, failure domains, team autonomy) are real but more nuanced in practice than theory suggests, and many can be achieved in monoliths

For CRUD applications, services should be sized around business entities—going too small leads to distributed transaction nightmares, while many 'must-haves' like separate data stores can be handled by convention in smaller teams
Stream-processing architectures genuinely benefit from easy service creation because developers constantly need to experiment on data pipelines without modifying existing code
Tooling investment decisions should be based on concrete math: cost of manual processes vs. automation maintenance, multiplied by expected frequency of use
money_quotes: As a concept, microservices feels like one of the most interesting folk architectures of the modern era. It's useful enough to be applied widely across different usage patterns and also vague enough to mean many different things.
Independent failure domains are sometimes more of a 'nice to have' than a necessity, and making the architecture truly account for this is not easy.

The challenge with monolith (and a related challenge with services in a monorepo) is that humans struggle to tangibly understand domains that are theoretically separate when they are presented as colocated by the source code.
It's better to go a little bit too big early than to go too small and end up having to implement significant distributed transactions.
Allowing teams to choose their ideal language, framework, and data store per service is also certainly not a must-have and in fact it's likely to be far more of a headache than a boon to your team.
This paragraph probably leads to sighs of exhaustion from anyone with experience doing data reconciliation.
When it becomes very easy to make anything a microservice, everything becomes a microservice, including things we would traditionally run as cron jobs.
posts.key_insight: Microservices is not a single pattern but multiple patterns serving different needs—CRUD applications benefit from cautious, entity-based extraction while stream-processing systems genuinely need easy service proliferation—and the right approach depends on your actual data flow, not industry folklore.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier argues that building engineering teams in the modern era requires balancing three key factors: money, purpose, and respect. She critiques Joel Spolsky's 2007 'nerd stereotypes' as outdated, noting the tech industry has dramatically expanded both in companies and in the diversity of people entering engineering. She emphasizes that underpaying engineers undermines their resilience to workplace problems, that purpose comes from involving engineers in business decisions rather than just technical work, and that respect means treating engineers as professionals who can contribute beyond coding—not coddling them with perks.
key_points: Money is foundational—underpaying engineers exacerbates every workplace irritation and undermines resilience during hard times
Most companies no longer have hard technical scaling problems, so purpose must come from connecting engineers to the business and product
Respect means challenging engineers to grow and partnering with them on business decisions, not pampering them with perks
Cross-functional teams that include engineering, product, marketing, and operations help engineers feel connected to the business
The talent pool has diversified significantly since 2007, with many engineers coming from non-traditional backgrounds
money_quotes: When you don't pay people well enough, you contribute to undermining their resilience in the face of problems at work.
The days when everyone had hard technical scaling problems have ended.
Leadership undermines their teams when they refuse to let engineers into the non-technical decision-making processes.
Our engineering teams are not overgrown children. They are not idiot-savants who can produce software but must be given sufficient cookies to do so.
Respect is not pampering, it is not treating the team like the stars of the show. Rather, respect is challenging the team to show up and grow up.
Engineers don't need to feel like the company royalty to be inspired to do good work, but they do need the opportunity to be treated like a partner.
Think of it as the baseline of Maslow's Hierarchy. Money does not solve all problems for most people, but lack of money exacerbates all irritations.
posts.key_insight: Motivating engineering teams requires paying market rate as a baseline, then offering purpose through business involvement and respect through professional partnership—not outdated notions of coddling technical talent.

posts.summary: The post introduces the concept of 'diddling' data - temporarily modifying a subset of pre-computed results without persisting changes back to the source. Fournier explains how this pattern, learned from finance systems, applies elegantly to personalization challenges like search results. Rather than personalizing across an entire massive dataset, you can apply transformations only to the top results that users will actually see. This approach balances the need for pre-computed efficiency with real-time customization, recognizing that humans only process a tiny surface area of any dataset at once.
key_points: Diddling means temporarily changing data within a limited scope to explore scenarios without persisting changes
Personalization can be framed as an enhancement layer on top of pre-computed results, not a fundamental recomputation
You only need to personalize the human-visible surface area, not the entire dataset
The pattern separates what must be indexed at scale from what can be computed on-the-fly
Pre-calculation handles the expensive work; diddling handles the customization cheaply
money_quotes: There are times when what you want to do is take persistent data, make a small change to it on the fly, and use the results of that change without necessarily persisting it back to the original data set.
Just because you have a list of thousands as your first pass of results doesn't mean you need to personalize across that whole data set to get the best results for the end user.
Personalization is often an enhancement on an underlying set of computed data, not the fundamental computation itself.
When your data gets in front of a human end-user, they are going to operate on only the tiny surface area they, as a human, can process at any one time.
You can apply different patterns on the fly to the human-visible surface area that would be too expensive to apply to the entire data set at large.
If your goal is to get the most personally relevant of the most generally relevant, you probably want to operate on the top of the generally-ordered list.
posts.key_insight: You can achieve effective personalization by applying lightweight transformations only to the visible surface of pre-computed results, rather than trying to personalize the entire dataset.

posts.summary: The post offers practical advice for giving constructive peer feedback by focusing on how colleagues get stuck or sidetracked. Fournier provides two lists: common distractions for individual contributors (like endless refactoring or helping others too much) and situations where they struggle (like finishing projects or asking for help). She notes that some people exhibit the opposite problem—sloppiness rather than getting stuck. Understanding these patterns is valuable for project planning, as it allows leaders to leverage strengths and mitigate weaknesses. The key insight is that everyone gets stuck sometimes, and self-awareness about one's own patterns enables growth or strategic avoidance.
key_points: Observing how peers get stuck is a 'super power' for giving useful feedback and for effective project planning
Common sidetracks include over-architecting, endless research, refactoring rabbit holes, and helping others at the expense of assigned work
Common stuck points include finishing the last 10-20% of projects, asking for help, working across teams, and saying no
Some people exhibit sloppiness rather than getting stuck—rushing to the next thing without finishing the current one
Self-awareness about your own stuck patterns enables you to either overcome them, avoid them, or apply extra diligence
money_quotes: Pay attention to how they get stuck.
Everyone has at least one area that they tend to get stuck on.
Noticing how people get stuck is a super power, and one that many great tech leads (and yes, managers) rely on to get big things done.
When you know how people get stuck, you can plan your projects to rely on people for their strengths and provide them help or even completely side-step their weaknesses.
All of us get stuck and sidetracked sometimes.
There's actually nothing particularly 'bad' about this.
Ask for help (far beyond the point they realized they were stuck and needed help)
posts.key_insight: Understanding how people get stuck—including yourself—is a leadership superpower that enables better feedback, smarter project planning, and more effective team collaboration.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier addresses why managers get stuck in their careers and fail to advance to managing managers. She identifies three key failure modes: failing to manage down (not delegating, not training successors, not pushing back on work), failing to manage up (not communicating effectively with leadership, complaining without solutions), and failing to manage sideways (not building peer relationships or creating compelling visions). The advice applies equally to senior individual contributors who struggle with the same non-technical skills. Getting unstuck requires honest self-assessment and actively seeking feedback from your manager about which areas need improvement.
key_points: Career progression requires mastery in three directions: managing down (delegation, training, process), managing up (communication, proactivity, professionalism), and managing sideways (peer relationships, cross-team leadership)
Being good at your current job doesn't automatically qualify you for the next level - you must demonstrate readiness for expanded scope
Working nights and weekends is usually a sign of poor delegation and time management, not dedication
Senior ICs face the same stuck points - technical excellence alone doesn't drive promotion beyond a certain level
Strategic thinking isn't just forming strategy; it's getting others excited about and bought into your ideas
money_quotes: Managers often believe that if they are handling the demands of managing their team, they should naturally be promoted to manage more people, bigger teams, teams of teams, as quickly as such an opportunity comes about.
If there are too many tasks that only you can complete, you have made yourself a key dependency for your team.
If your team is completely overwhelmed with work, well, it's partially your fault.
Instead of complaining, volunteer to lead the initiative to fix that team-wide problem. Bring problems and solutions.
Many people think that strategic thinking starts and stops with forming the strategy itself, but getting people around you excited by your ideas is critical to achieving them.
Ultimately, if you want to manage managers, a manager should want to work for you.
Few senior ICs get promoted beyond a point on the strength of their ideas and code alone.
posts.key_insight: Advancing in management requires demonstrating capability in three directions simultaneously - effectively scaling yourself through your team, proactively managing upward communication and taking on challenges, and building peer relationships that make others want to work for you.

posts.summary: Fournier identifies a common management anti-pattern: asking questions while simultaneously revealing your preferred answer, which shuts down genuine discussion. She recognizes this habit in herself while commenting on a strategy document and reflects on why it's destructive. The core problem is that stating preferences upfront makes people unlikely to share dissenting views, especially with senior leaders. She advocates for 'getting curious' as a practice—making deliberate space for information before jumping to conclusions. Good decision-making requires understanding nuances from all angles, which is impossible when people fear contradicting you.
key_points: Asking a question while stating your preferred answer cuts off genuine discussion and discourages honest responses
The more senior you become, the harder it is for people to feel comfortable disagreeing with you openly—this is rational self-preservation, not weakness
Highly opinionated engineers must practice making space for information rather than jumping to conclusions to grow as leaders
One of the less-obvious ways we intimidate people is by offering opinions too early, before gathering all the information
Written communication allows you to catch and edit these habits; verbal conversation requires more deliberate practice
money_quotes: I pretend to ask a question but then make it clear that only one answer can be right.
By stating my preference up-front I cut off discussion.
The more senior you become, the harder it is for people to feel comfortable disagreeing with you openly. That is not a sign of weakness on their part!
Most people with any sense of self-preservation know that saying the wrong thing to the wrong person can have negative consequences.

Lucky for you, as the manager, people are going to listen to what you have to say. Unlucky for you, as the manager, if you don't make space for them to say things that you disagree with, you are unlikely to hear important details.
One of the less-obvious ways we make people afraid is by offering our opinions too early, without taking the time to get the rest of the information.
Get curious. You already know there's something you don't totally understand, so hold on stating your opinions until you're sure you've gotten as much information as possible.
posts.key_insight: Leaders who offer their opinions before fully listening create an environment where people withhold important information out of self-preservation, leading to worse decisions.

posts.summary: Alignment in organizations means being in agreement with what the company values and needs, and many senior individual contributors stall in their careers because they fail to recognize they've fallen out of alignment. Early in your career, alignment comes naturally from executing assigned tasks well, but at senior levels, you must actively discover and pursue work that matters to the organization. The shift requires moving from solving the hardest problems to identifying the right problems to solve. This means talking to people, understanding their challenges, and betting on solutions that are both feasible and valued. Success at senior levels comes from making wise bets—knowing which work will actually pay off for both you and the company.
key_points: Early career alignment is passive (do assigned work well), but senior alignment requires actively finding important problems to solve
The subtle misalignment trap is wanting your fun, interesting work to magically be the important work without doing the legwork to verify it
Finding alignment requires talking to many people, listening to their problems, and betting on solutions that are both feasible and seen as important
An alternative path is taking responsibility for unglamorous but clearly important broken areas, though this carries burnout risk
Senior promotions come from making wise bets—being attuned enough to organizational needs to know the payout of what you're pursuing
money_quotes: The more subtle way that most of us get out of alignment is by being unwilling to admit that we don't understand what's important to the company.
We want to do things that are fun and challenging and interesting to us and have that work out to be the important thing.
The hardest part is now identifying the right problem to solve, instead of solving the hardest problem.
At a certain level, creating the alignment between projects that are interesting for you and what is needed by the company is a big part of your job.
I often say that at more senior levels you get promoted for making wise bets, and really what that means is that you are smart enough to know the payout for what you're pursuing.
You get jealous that people aren't asking for your advice before they build things, because clearly you know better!

The risk you take here is that you either burn out fighting to fix a situation that is really about people and organizational issues that are beyond your scope, or you pick a broken area that no one really cares about fixing.
posts.key_insight: At senior levels, career advancement depends not on solving hard problems but on developing the judgment to identify which problems actually matter to the organization and betting your energy accordingly.

posts.summary: Effective delegation requires managers to resist the instinct to be 'helpful' by taking over work from their direct reports. Drawing on the classic HBR article about 'subordinate-imposed time,' Fournier explains how managers inadvertently absorb tasks that belong to their team members when they volunteer to make decisions, provide first drafts, or insist on final reviews. The solution is to keep initiative with the team member while providing guidance through structured touchpoints like 1-1s. This approach not only protects the manager's time but also develops the team's skills and autonomy.
key_points: Being 'helpful' as a manager often means taking over work that should remain with your direct reports, which hurts both parties
Experienced managers rarely accept offers of direct intervention, instead presenting their own approach and only escalating when their efforts fail
The key distinction is between providing guidance/feedback versus taking ownership of the work itself
Most delegation touchpoints can happen within existing 1-1 meetings, freeing up unscheduled time for other priorities
Team members won't learn to make good decisions if managers always provide a safety net or take over the hard parts
money_quotes: Without effective delegation, you fall victim to micromanaging your team, losing control of your time, and eventually failing to put yourself in a position where you can take on more and lead bigger things.
I want my team to be happy and successful, and I want to feel useful and needed.
They aren't going to learn how to make good decisions, set good goals, or write effective docs, if you are always there providing a safety net or taking over the hard work from them.
So the next time you find yourself tempted to volunteer to take over responsibility for something from someone who reports to you, pause.
Instead of taking it over, ask them what they think the next steps should be.
You owe it to them to stop taking over their work in the name of helpfulness.
posts.key_insight: True helpfulness as a manager means keeping ownership with your team members and providing guidance through structured touchpoints, not taking over their work.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier argues that while engineering managers have diverse strengths, the non-negotiable skill is creating highly productive teams. She identifies three core competencies: breaking down project scope to ship frequently, balancing delivery with sustaining engineering to manage technical debt, and ruthless prioritization. The post emphasizes that failing in these areas leads to disengaged teams, burnout, and purposelessness. While acknowledging other management skills matter, she maintains that delivery focus is fundamental to the role.
key_points: Engineering productivity is the one non-optional characteristic of great engineering managers, regardless of their other strengths
Project management should not be handed off to professional PMs for work within a single team - it's essential to engineering leadership development
Technical debt 'inevitably comes due unless you declare bankruptcy' - the investment in sustaining engineering depends on future certainty
Teams that don't ship become disengaged and miss the positive feedback of seeing work come to fruition
Nurturing healthy teams and helping them deliver their best work are interconnected, not competing priorities
money_quotes: A great engineering manager is capable of creating a highly productive engineering team.
If you are not great at getting your team to be productive, this is a critical growth opportunity.
Ask any engineering manager at a startup what one of their most dreaded questions is, and they will almost certainly mention 'why isn't it done yet?'

This is why I think project management is such an important part of engineering leadership development, and why I hate to hand it off to professional project managers for work that doesn't cross teams or organizations.
There's a reason we call it 'technical debt' and that is because it inevitably comes due, unless you declare bankruptcy.
Teams who don't ship are usually disengaged and rarely get the positive feedback of seeing their work come to fruition.
If your team isn't shipping, you're not doing your job.
Nurturing a safe and healthy team helps your team do their best work, and helping them identify and deliver that best work is a key part of keeping them healthy.
posts.key_insight: Engineering managers must master productivity and delivery - without it, even the best people management skills leave teams disengaged and purposeless.

posts.summary: This post explores how managers can demonstrate readiness to advance from managing individual contributors to managing other managers. Fournier argues that aspiring leaders must first shift their focus from technical details to people, teams, and strategy. Success depends on three key factors: effectively scaling your current team, being someone other managers would want to work for, and being trusted by senior leadership to operate independently. The post emphasizes that these skills can be developed even without a larger team by focusing on organizational contributions, peer relationships, and professional presence. Ultimately, advancement requires honest self-assessment and openness to feedback rather than assuming you're already qualified.
key_points: Ready leaders look outward at people and teams and forward at strategy, rather than down into technical details
Scaling successfully as a manager means your team ships quality work and new hires onboard effectively—without this, growth opportunities go elsewhere
Managers want bosses who coach without micromanaging, provide context for decisions, and create room for autonomy and mistakes
Building collaborative peer relationships across teams signals leadership readiness more than individual team performance alone
Being coachable and acting on feedback matters more than experience when senior leaders choose who to promote
money_quotes: You need to develop the ability to leverage your attention and time through more people and projects.
Be honest with yourself: Are you ready to spend less time in the technical details? If not, you are probably not going to enjoy managing managers.
Most of us want to promote from within, but when your team isn't running effectively, it doesn't make us want to give you more.
Managers want a boss who can teach us things. We want coaching on how to be better, without being micromanaged.
If you're tempted to say they're all 'too junior' to be leaders it's likely that you haven't developed the coaching skills yet that you will need to manage another manager successfully.
So many line managers think that they are temporarily embarrassed CTOs, and don't realize that they are lacking in many critical skills.

If you bristle at every bit of corrective feedback, if you have an outsized ego that everyone can see, or even if you simply never actually act on the feedback that you get, you're not going to be top of the list when the opportunities come about.
Your well-meaning advice will come across as a signal that you think you could do their job better, and that rarely goes over well.
posts.key_insight: Readiness to manage managers is demonstrated not by technical excellence but by your ability to develop leaders, build peer relationships, operate independently, and remain genuinely coachable.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier argues that manager READMEs—documents where managers describe their working style, preferences, and flaws to their teams—are fundamentally misguided despite good intentions. She contends that these documents are inherently self-serving, that managers lack the self-awareness to accurately describe themselves, and that publicly declaring personality flaws doesn't excuse bad behavior. The power differential between managers and reports makes it nearly impossible for team members to call out hypocrisy when managers fail to live up to their stated values. Trust is built through consistent ethical behavior over time, not through documentation. While such introspective exercises can be valuable for personal coaching or therapy, sharing them with direct reports creates unintended negative consequences.
key_points: Manager READMEs are inherently self-serving and cannot build trust—trust comes from consistent ethical behavior over time, not documentation
Most people lack the self-awareness to accurately describe themselves, making these documents unreliable (the 'Dunning-Kruger of self-awareness')
The power differential makes it nearly impossible for direct reports to give honest feedback or call out contradictions between stated values and actual behavior
Publicly declaring your flaws doesn't excuse bad behavior—if you know you have problematic behaviors, do the work to change them instead of warning people
These exercises are valuable for personal introspection, coaching, or therapy, but the accountability partner cannot be someone who reports to you
money_quotes: There is no way to write these and not be self-serving.
I know myself well enough to know that I might usually behave in alignment with certain personal values and expectations, but I will break that alignment at the worst possible times.

It is incredibly difficult to tell someone that the thing they believe about themselves enough to publicly declare is, in fact, not true. It's hard to tell your partner that, it's hard to tell your friends that, and it's basically impossible to tell your boss.

If you want to build trust, you do that by showing up, talking to your team both individually and as a team, and behaving in an ethical, reliable manner. Over, and over, and over again. You don't get it from writing a doc about how you deserve their trust.
Don't put them out there for your team to praise you for the intention to do the work, just do it.
Writing them down feels good, like you've been honest and vulnerable and no one can be surprised when you behave badly, after all you warned them! But it does not excuse these bad behaviors.
Good intentions don't just magically make bad ideas turn good.
You're not a computer.
posts.key_insight: Manager READMEs fail because they attempt to shortcut trust-building through documentation, but trust can only be earned through consistent behavior over time, and the power differential makes it impossible for direct reports to hold managers accountable to their stated values.

posts.summary: The post addresses a common struggle among high-achieving professionals: the compulsion to fix every problem they encounter, regardless of whether it's their responsibility. Fournier shares her personal journey of chasing positions of power (manager, engineering leader, CTO) believing each would finally give her the ability to fix everything, only to end up burned out. She provides a practical five-step framework for deciding when to engage with a problem: determine ownership, gather information through conversations, plan the fix, execute it, and reflect on the cost. The core message is that no role exists where you can fix everything, and the exhausting pursuit of that ideal is a trap. She concludes that if you're constantly battling problems you can't fix, it's time to find a company whose culture better matches your values.
key_points: There is no job—not manager, not VP, not CTO, not even founder—where you can fix all the problems
Before taking on someone else's problem, verify it actually affects your work significantly, not just that they're 'doing it wrong'
The energy required to solve unowned problems (talking to stakeholders, building consensus, executing) is almost always more than you expect
Feeling tired at the prospect of the political work required to fix something is a signal to let it go
Chronic frustration with unfixable problems is a sign you need to find a company with better cultural fit, not more authority
money_quotes: There is no place you can go where you can control everything and fix all the problems, no matter how much you get promoted.
Tend your own garden first.
Does it bother you because they're doing it wrong, or does it actually, really, significantly make it harder for you to do your job?
If you feel tired by the idea of talking to all people, stop! This is a sign that you should not pick this battle!
Your goal is to convince everyone that they want you to solve the problem.
Unfortunately, the fix will almost certainly take a lot longer than you're thinking it will take.
Your job really shouldn't be all or even mostly about battles.
Life is so much more fun when you have people around you that you trust to solve problems, even the problems you have a lot of opinions about.
posts.key_insight: The compulsion to fix every problem you see—regardless of ownership—leads to burnout; wisdom lies in recognizing which battles are truly yours and finding environments where you can trust others to handle the rest.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier explores the unique challenges of product management for internal platform teams, which face a small captive audience that's hard to truly empathize with. She argues that platform teams often fail by building thoughtlessly without clear customer focus. The post offers four strategies: assimilating successful internal tools built by other teams, partnering to prototype solutions, prioritizing migration strategies early, and avoiding the trap of over-building like big tech companies. She concludes that great platform teams tell a clear story about their products and maintain strong partner relationships rather than chasing cool technology.
key_points: Internal platform teams face unique product challenges: small customer base makes A/B testing ineffective, captive audiences lead to ignored adoption metrics, and being deep in technical details makes it hard to empathize with users
Platform teams often end up with 'several overlapping half-finished products' because they assumed captive audiences guaranteed success
Taking over successful tools built by other teams is a valid strategy—you inherit a satisfied customer and proven problem identification
Migration strategy must be a primary part of product planning, not an afterthought—carrots like efficiency, better SLOs, or security are needed to drive adoption
Platform teams should exhaust alternatives before building, as imitating big company systems often encodes assumptions that don't fit your context
money_quotes: A captive audience leads us to believe that basic metrics for customer adoption are not interesting, which in turn leads us to ignore them, sometimes to our peril.

When platform teams build to be building, especially when they have grand visions of complex end goals with few intermediary states, you end up with products that are confusing, overengineered, and far from beloved.
Don't be ashamed to take over a system from a team that built it with themselves in mind, if that system seems to be the right general concept for the wider company.
You already have a reasonably satisfied customer to start with!
The product work is not 'tell everyone you have kubernetes now and they have to use it.'
It is not good product management to say 'Google does it, therefore we should.'
Without a clear strategy for showing impact and value, you end up overlooked and understaffed, and no amount of cool new technology will solve that problem.
posts.key_insight: Platform teams fail when they build for the sake of building rather than maintaining genuine customer focus and clear migration strategies for their captive but easily-ignored audience.

posts.summary: The post argues that managers who successfully turn around struggling teams focus on small, tactical improvements rather than grand transformations. While many managers respond to team dysfunction by proposing sweeping changes—technical rewrites, bold product visions, or wholesale talent replacement—these approaches typically fail. Great managers instead identify and fix small problems like unclear on-call schedules, unfocused priorities, or cumbersome processes. These incremental wins build momentum (the 'flywheel'), making the team happier and more productive, which in turn makes the bigger changes possible and sustainable.
key_points: Teams stuck in ruts often have managers who keep promising the next big thing will fix everything, but real improvement requires a different approach
Three common failure modes: technical managers who want to rewrite everything, product managers who paint grand visions without execution paths, and talent-focused managers who blame the people
Great managers start by fixing small things—on-call schedules, project prioritization, unclear responsibilities—that reduce friction and enable faster decisions
The flywheel effect means small wins compound: a productive team is easier to hire for, easier to demand better product direction for, and more capable of tackling architectural problems
Big transformational changes should come after the team has momentum, not as a first act
money_quotes: Have you ever worked on a team that felt like it was just stuck in a rut? Somehow things were always just one fix away from improving: the next project, the next quarter, the next hire, this would turn the situation around.
When everything is going well, all a decent manager has to do is not screw things up, and it's not always easy to tell on paper whether a manager is merely good or truly excellent.
But ask a manager about how they've gotten a team out of a rut, and now you start to hear about a real, common situation that a manager can make or break.
My experience is that most of these managers, in these situations, will fail. I have personally failed in all of these ways at least once in my career. But we won't admit we failed.
These little things start to build up steam. Now the team feels less burdened by unwieldy processes, and starts to make decisions faster.
It's easier to hire when the team is engaged and excited to add new talent. And it's much, much easier to fix a bad architecture when the team is able to ship changes.
When you find yourself in a rut, remember that you don't have to solve the root cause of everything wrong with the team as a first act.
Big things start small, so don't forget to sweat the small stuff.
posts.key_insight: The path to transforming a struggling team runs through small tactical wins that build momentum, not through grand visions that demand a team already be high-functioning to execute.

posts.summary: Fournier argues that platform developers and software engineers can drive cultural change not just through top-down leadership mandates, but by embedding best practices directly into the tools and templates they create. While senior leadership can change culture by shifting rewards and priorities, this approach is slow and can have unintended consequences like making teams risk-averse. A more effective lever is product features: when starting templates include tests, monitoring, and security practices, engineers naturally adopt these patterns through copy-paste-modify behavior.
