The key insight is that making the right thing easy is more effective than enforcing compliance.
key_points: Leadership-driven culture change works by shifting rewards and focus, but is slow and can overcorrect, pushing away valuable engineers
Engineers naturally copy-paste-modify existing code, so whatever patterns exist in templates and examples will propagate throughout the codebase
Pre-integrated features (like Dropwizard bundling metrics, monitoring, and healthchecks) make services better from day one without requiring cultural mandates
Platform developers have significant power to shape engineering culture by baking best practices into infrastructure and tooling
Security and testing can be enforced through tooling rather than relying on 'good citizenship' of development teams
money_quotes: You don't just want to create a heavyweight process, you want engineers to start to value these things enough that they don't need a process to enforce them.

One of my earliest professional software lessons was that when you set up a codebase full of tests, other engineers are likely to write tests for their code because there will be lots of examples for how to test.
People are most likely to take an existing thing and tweak it into a new thing that does what they need, and in the process they will take the good and bad from that existing thing.
Pre-integration of stuff that you really need to run a service well means that your services are better from day one.
You can also go quite far by providing tools and platforms that have good security practices baked in to them, so that you're not relying on the good citizenship of your development team.
Developers have more power than they imagine to change the engineering culture around them.
posts.key_insight: The most effective way to change engineering culture is not through process enforcement or leadership mandates, but by embedding best practices directly into the tools, templates, and platforms that engineers use daily.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier argues that while 'Choose Boring Technology' has become accepted wisdom, engineering leaders need to extend this principle to planning itself. She observes that Platform Engineering teams especially suffer when management relies on vision alone rather than concrete, predictable plans. Novel technology initiatives require boring, incremental planning to succeed—starting with proof of concepts, documenting learnings, and communicating clear roadmaps. Good strategy isn't about razzle-dazzle innovation but about identifying core problems so solutions become obvious. Leaders who fail to make boring plans create unnecessary stress and anxiety for their teams, while those who embrace predictable planning free their organizations from the tyranny of uncertainty.
key_points: Vision alone is insufficient—'hire smart people and get out of their way' breaks down for platform teams where downstream dependencies create high stakes
Platform teams often absorb innovation tokens forced upon them by other teams building shadow infrastructure, then inherit operational expectations without proper groundwork
Boring plans mean starting with proof of concepts, documenting learnings iteratively, and communicating predictable roadmaps to both team and users
Good strategy makes solutions obvious and simple; strategies relying on 'unproven magic bullets' are bad strategies
Constantly changing plans signal either false certainty or insufficient research—both waste time and stress teams
money_quotes: Why isn't this boring?
It is a common belief in engineering that, with a clear enough vision, the rest of the pieces of work will fall into place.
Novel Technology Deserves Boring Plans
Strategy is not in the business of razzle-dazzle, it's in the business of getting to the core of the issues so that the solution becomes simple and obvious.
Plans that are formed around hope are failing plans; hope is not a plan.
Plans that change constantly are failing plans.
When your plans are constantly changing, it is a sign that you either are making plans that express a certainty you don't have, or you haven't done your research to get the right certainty in place.
You owe it your teams, and to your users, to free them from the tyranny and stress of uncertainty.
posts.key_insight: When working with novel or high-stakes technology, leaders must compensate by making their plans predictable, incremental, and boring—because hope is not a plan and constant change signals insufficient preparation.

posts.summary: This post provides a practical framework for managers to determine performance ratings fairly and thoughtfully. Fournier advocates for combining three approaches: goal-setting with tiered expectations (must-achieve, stretch, and moonshot goals), evaluating against consistent role-specific attributes, and applying personal judgment. She emphasizes that no perfect algorithm exists for rating people, and managers must reconcile objective data with their gut reactions to uncover hidden biases. The process requires starting early with clear expectations and being prepared to defend ratings during calibration exercises.
key_points: Goal-setting should use tiered categories (must-achieve, stretch, moonshot) to create clear benchmarks for different rating levels
Evaluating people against consistent attributes helps identify strengths and weaknesses, but people don't fit neatly into boxes
Senior managers should be judged partly on their ability to anticipate and course-correct when teams face challenges
Comparing data-driven assessments against gut reactions reveals both rating biases and unstated role requirements
The rating process starts long before evaluation time, with clear goal-setting and role alignment
money_quotes: As with all things in the world of people management, there is no perfect algorithm you can apply to ensure total fairness and accuracy.
Only you can tell the difference between a manager who was blindsided by events and a manager who just failed to plan well, or who couldn't course correct their team effectively.
The most interesting and useful part of this exercise is comparing what you get from this data against your gut reaction to the rating you think someone should get.
You do not want to set yourself up to deny good ratings to people doing good work when they don't fit perfectly in a box.
If you are open-minded, the data will show you that you're off both high and low in different cases.

Sometimes, it will force you to acknowledge your own bias: why do I always want to give this kind of person a lower rating even though objectively their work looks as good as this other kind of person?
posts.key_insight: Fair performance ratings require combining structured frameworks with honest self-examination of your own judgment and biases.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier presents a comprehensive list of non-technical skills that distinguish senior engineers from their less experienced peers. The post argues that advancing beyond mid-level engineering requires mastering interpersonal, communication, and organizational skills that have nothing to do with writing code. These skills span running meetings effectively, mentoring others, navigating organizational politics, and influencing without authority. The list progresses from tactical skills like writing design docs to strategic abilities like convincing management to invest in technical projects.
key_points: Running meetings effectively means facilitating outcomes, not dominating the conversation
Senior engineers must learn to influence and persuade without formal authority over other teams or engineers
Emotional intelligence is critical: making others feel appreciated, not threatened, and not rolling your eyes at less technical colleagues
Career advancement requires proactively finding work and building political capital rather than waiting for assignments
Teaching and mentoring others—including helping them get promoted—becomes a core responsibility at senior levels
money_quotes: How to run a meeting, and no, being the person who talks the most in the meeting is not the same thing as running it
How to indulge a senior manager who wants to talk about technical stuff that they don't really understand, without rolling your eyes or making them feel stupid
How to get another engineer to do something for you by asking for help in a way that makes them feel appreciated
How to lead a project even though you don't manage any of the people working on the project
How to listen to other engineers' ideas without feeling threatened
How to give up your baby, that project that you built into something great, so you can do something else
How to repeat yourself enough that people start to listen
How to find interesting work on your own, instead of waiting for someone to bring it to you
posts.key_insight: The skills that make someone a senior engineer are predominantly interpersonal and organizational, not technical—success at higher levels depends on influence, communication, and emotional intelligence.

posts.summary: New managers often inadvertently stifle the career growth of individual contributors by holding onto technical work and decision-making authority they should be delegating. The post identifies five common pitfalls: doing all technical design work, hoarding project management, neglecting non-technical feedback, keeping information to themselves, and focusing on personal output over team development. Fournier argues that managers must shift from additive value (their own contributions) to multiplicative value (amplifying their team's output). The key insight is that new managers need to actively create space for ICs to grow by stepping back from the technical leadership role they recently held, which benefits both the IC's career trajectory and the manager's ability to scale.
key_points: New managers must deliberately step back from technical design work to create growth opportunities for ICs, even when those ICs lack equivalent skills
Teaching ICs project management is painful but essential for their career advancement and for the manager's ability to scale
Managers who only give technical feedback reinforce the perception that management is the only path to authority
Information hoarding turns ICs into 'doers' while managers become 'deciders,' undermining IC ownership and engagement
A manager's output is measured by team results, not personal contributions—the goal is multiplicative rather than additive value
money_quotes: If you respond by filling in for their skill gaps, you are going to quickly hit two problems. First, you won't be able to scale because you'll be too busy doing technical stuff to take on a bigger team. Second, you won't be able to scale because you won't have a person to whom you can delegate.
Teaching someone how to run a project is painful, and they will often say that they don't want to do the work, don't want to learn it, and make your life difficult in the process.
The result is the impression that management is the way to have technical authority over a group, which leads ICs to wonder what the technical track is even for.
When you don't give your team the context for the work and just pass on tasks and work items to them, you make it clear that they are simply 'doers' and your job is the job of 'decider.'
If it's your code that gets you over the finish line for every project, you aren't providing multiplicative value for the team, you're providing the additive value of your work as an engineer.
New managers, make sure that you aren't trying to be a senior engineer who has direct reports.
posts.key_insight: New managers must transition from being high-performing individual contributors to creating leverage through team development, which means deliberately stepping back from technical work to make room for others to grow.

posts.summary: Software engineers are drawn to systematic, structural approaches to management, but rigid organizational structures often fail when applied to real teams. While rules of thumb like optimal team sizes (7-8 direct reports) have value, experienced managers know that living organizations constantly shift due to departures, hiring bursts, and individual needs. Applying engineering concepts like APIs to team interactions ignores how humans actually work together—trading favors, exploiting informal channels, and adapting to circumstances. Leaders who focus too heavily on optimizing systems risk losing good people who don't fit perfectly into their structures. The key is balancing organizational principles with flexibility, accepting imperfect structures to retain people over the long term.
key_points: Engineering managers don't scale well beyond 7-8 direct reports, but organizations with only 2-3 reports create limbo states between IC and manager roles
Clean organizational tree structures quickly fall apart due to departures, sticky manager-team relationships, and uneven hiring patterns
Applying engineering analogies like APIs to team interactions fails because humans will always find and exploit informal channels (Hyrum's Law applies to organizations)
Optimizing for system efficiency can inadvertently create friction for people who don't fit the structure, causing attrition
Startup experience can mislead managers into thinking they can perfectly tune structures, but stable organizations require accepting imperfection to retain people over years
money_quotes: Software engineers are attracted to formulas, algorithms, and structures.
The teams need to have some coherence of vision and purpose; hiring is never a steady drip but comes and goes in bursts.

APIs are a fine concept but the idea that team interactions can be strictly defined by an API is as laughable as the idea that programmers won't figure out a way to exploit every undocumented feature you provide in one.
Hyrum's Law applies as much to engineering organizations as it does to software.
When you see the world in systems, interactions, and efficiencies, you can trick yourself into believing that the humans inside of these systems will be happier if the system is well-organized.
The people in the system are much trickier than you are imagining, and the structure that accounts for their skills and happiness will have organic features as much as centrally-planned ones.

When you are thinking in terms of years and not months, it becomes important to accept that you may hang out in imperfect structures for many months or even longer, because you are trying to hang onto people for years.
Listen to your people, and don't be afraid to break your perfect structures to accommodate their needs.
posts.key_insight: Organizational structures should be treated as living, imperfect systems that bend to accommodate people's needs rather than rigid frameworks that force people to conform.

posts.summary: Fournier argues that adding product managers to infrastructure organizations won't magically create a product culture—the entire engineering culture must change. Infrastructure teams excel at cost management and technical depth but typically treat internal users as a captive audience rather than customers. The transformation requires updating support models, interview processes, recognition systems, and how engineers spend their time. She warns against over-relying on project managers and emphasizes that engineers must own migrations and customer relationships directly. The shift parallels the DevOps transformation: it asks more of engineers but delivers higher-quality outcomes.
key_points: Product managers alone cannot fix a culture problem—engineering teams must feel ownership for delivering great products to customers
Infrastructure teams must change how they provide support, with engineers regularly answering questions to understand customer pain
Recognition and reward systems must value usability work and customer empathy, not just hard technical problems
Heavy reliance on project managers masks poor migration UX and prevents engineers from building automation that would help customers
Engineers will spend more time talking to customers and less time purely writing code, with an up-front productivity cost that pays off over time
money_quotes: You can't just rub product managers on it and call it a day.

If the engineering teams don't feel a sense of ownership for delivering a great product to their customers, product managers are unlikely to close that gap, and they will more likely turn into glorified backlog groomers than true product leaders.
The ticket system black hole is a great way to make your customers feel more like a burden than a focus.
If you have someone you can't trust to engage productively in help situations, watch out, because this person is probably not building products that are easy to use.
If you only promote people who solve big technical problems, you're going to have a hard time retaining the people who do the work to smooth out the usability edges.
Yes, migrations are part of your UX!
It is simply not much fun to have an antagonistic relationship with your users, and to feel like you are deep inside of an us-vs-them dynamic with colleagues.
Engineers in a product-focused organization do not build software without consideration of the users of that software.
posts.key_insight: Transforming infrastructure organizations into product-focused teams requires changing engineering culture itself—how you hire, reward, support customers, and structure work—not just adding product managers to existing processes.

posts.summary: The post argues that senior engineers who want to advance their careers must stop waiting to be handed increasingly difficult problems and instead learn to identify problems themselves by examining their work in its 'next larger context.' Fournier explains that career progression beyond senior engineer rarely comes from solving harder assigned problems, but from finding where business opportunities intersect with technology solutions. She provides three detailed examples—a bespoke storage system, an inventory management system, and a developer tools team—showing how engineers can expand their perspective to find impactful work. The key is to look just one level broader than your current scope to discover adjacent opportunities, rather than waiting for projects to be assigned to you.
key_points: Career advancement past senior engineer rarely comes from being handed harder problems—it comes from identifying problems yourself
The key skill is finding where business opportunities intersect with what technology can solve
Looking at the 'next larger context' means understanding how your system serves users and fits into broader trends
Sometimes the advanced move is simplification or deprecation, not building something more complex
You should only look one context bigger to avoid overengineering—complex systems must evolve from simple ones
money_quotes: It is exceedingly rare that the path past senior engineer comes from being handed increasingly hard problems.
The blending of these two things, business opportunities and technology, is where you need to turn your eye, senior engineer.
Sometimes the advanced move is not to build a more complex thing, but to enable the company to embrace the broader trends.
You will only do that well if you understand why you need to differentiate and how to make the case for that based on the understanding of the larger context around your system, namely, its users.
Don't wait for the next project to come to you, seek it out by looking at the slightly bigger picture.
You look only one context bigger because it is easy to overdo things and violate our 'evolve complex systems from simple systems' principle when you try to go too broad.
posts.key_insight: Senior engineers advance not by solving harder assigned problems, but by proactively identifying opportunities one context larger than their current scope—where business needs and technical possibilities intersect.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier reflects on publishing Rent the Runway's engineering career ladder 10 years ago, one of the first public ladders with clear IC/management distinctions. She shares the open-source motivation behind publishing it and notes how the practice has become widespread across the industry. The post offers practical advice for organizations writing their own ladders, emphasizing the importance of details, honesty about career paths, and avoiding pure checklist approaches. She concludes by questioning whether ladders remain relevant in an era of 'flat organization' backlash, but argues that human nature and the need for transparent, equitable structures means this work still matters.
key_points: Publishing the RTR engineering ladder in 2015 helped spark an industry-wide movement toward transparent career frameworks
Career ladders should be more prescriptive than horoscopes - details and long-form explanations help flesh out expectations
Promotions should reflect organizational needs, not just individual skill checklists
Not every job family needs a ladder from bottom to top - be honest about realistic career paths
Despite 'flat organization' trends, human instincts toward hierarchy and the tyranny of structurelessness make career ladders still valuable
money_quotes: As an open source person, I believe in the value of sharing not only ideas but actual work for others to use, build upon, and expand, for the greater benefit of the tech community.

Most people want a starting point to play off of when designing organizational processes as much as they want one for technical work. Even if all the starting point inspires is 'ew, not this!', it gives you something to riff off of.
Your ladder should be more prescriptive than a horoscope.
Promotions are as much about the needs of the organization as the skills of the employees.
Don't promise people that they can become the VP of X just because some people do job X.
I'm not convinced that we'll overcome the human instinct to gather in groups and create leaders of those groups, and fight one another for power of those groups.
The Tyranny of Structurelessness has yet to be overcome.
posts.key_insight: Career ladders remain essential infrastructure for equitable organizations because human nature inevitably creates hierarchies - the choice is whether those structures are transparent and intentional or hidden and arbitrary.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier argues that platform teams rarely drive novel innovation, instead absorbing external innovations or internal team creations. However, this doesn't diminish the need for platform strategy—rather, platform teams serve as the fulcrum for architectural evolution across companies. She contends that platform leadership strategy involves watching industry trends and making incremental improvements that enable natural evolution. The key insight is that 'boring but reliable forward progress' is itself strategic, even if it's not revolutionary innovation.
key_points: Platform teams typically adopt external innovations or absorb platform-like work created by application teams, rather than driving novel innovation themselves
Every major technology shift (containerization, cloud, microservices, observability) has required platform-level support to succeed
Platform strategy means watching the state of the art and making tools available that encourage natural evolution, not forcing overnight transformations
'Strategy' and 'Vision' are conflated in ways that cause people to overlook steady, incremental progress as strategic work
When platform leaders execute well over years, product teams gain flexibility and power to innovate without going it alone
money_quotes: Platform teams are often the fulcrum that drives important architectural evolution across companies.
You're watching where the state of the art is going, and thinking about what makes sense to introduce or evolve so that you can take advantage of that over time.

You're not forcing everyone to move from bare metal hardware to kubernetes overnight, but you're making the tools to support bare metal to virtualization/containers available, easy to use, and cost-effective.
This is the engine of baseline modernization that needs constant feeding to keep your technology stack from stagnating.
'Strategy' and 'Vision' are so often conflated in peoples' minds that this kind of boring but reliable forward progress is overlooked as strategic.
Platform leaders, don't feel bad if you're not at the forefront of innovation!
You don't have to drive the next innovation to be strategic, you just need to make it easy for others to get there.
posts.key_insight: Platform leadership strategy isn't about driving revolutionary innovation—it's about steady, incremental modernization that makes it easy for others to innovate.

posts.summary: Camille Fournier revisits her earlier critique of manager READMEs, arguing they remain ineffective tools for senior leaders. The core problem is that executives need to quickly understand what matters across many people with different communication styles, but standardized company tooling provides only lowest-common-denominator usefulness. READMEs are lazy and create a you-focused mindset that encourages information bubbles. Instead, she advocates for lightweight templates and ceremonies that show what you care about organizationally, while resisting the urge to processify everything. The key is driving clarity through selective standardization that benefits the collective, not just accommodating individual leader preferences.
key_points: Manager READMEs create a problematic you-focused mindset that encourages information bubbles and managing up to personality quirks
Standard company reporting (Jira, OKRs, dashboards) provides lowest-common-denominator usefulness that obscures as much as it reveals
The better alternative is lightweight templates and ceremonies for information sharing, discussion, and decision-making
Good templates show what information should be collected without dictating exactly how to collect it
Not everything should have a fixed process—some discussions need open-ended exploration rather than formal structure
money_quotes: READMEs are also rather lazy. There's a kernel of truth in their purpose: we want people to focus certain types of communication on what we believe is most valuable.
It creates a focus on you as the person to be managed up to. Your personality must be accommodated, your preferences honored.
I want to hear what I care about, yes, but I don't want to live in an information bubble either.
A big part of the job of senior/executive management is setting standards for doing certain types of work.
Dashboards are like alerts and tests in this way, they tend to catch what you know could go wrong, but rarely the surprise problems that lead to big incidents. Necessary, but insufficient.
Start simple and add on new details or processes only when it's clear you have a widespread gap.
Lead your organization by showing them where to spend their time and focus as a collective good, not just good for you.
posts.key_insight: Senior leaders should drive clarity through selective lightweight templates and ceremonies that benefit the organization collectively, rather than manager READMEs that center individual preferences and create information bubbles.
